subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements: 1. The Governor of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is appointed by the Central Government. 2. Certain provisions in the Constitution of India give the Central Government the right to issue directions to the RBI in public interest. 3. The Governor of the RBI draws his power from the RBI Act. Which of the above statements are correct?
context:
By: Basava Uppin	RBI: Analysis of PYQs

1
2
3

Prelims 2013

Prelims 2025
Year Wise Breakup of Questions 	Topic Wise Breakup of Questions
Role of RBI 	Current Developments

By: Basava Uppin
Prelims 2021
Consider the following statements:
1. 	The Governor of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is appointed by the
Central Government.
2. 	Certain 	provisions 	in 	the 	Constitution 	of 	India 	give 	the 	Central
Government the right to issue directions to the RBI in public interest.
3. 	The Governor of the RBI draws his power from the RBI Act.
Which of the above statements are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only 	(b) 2 and 3 only 	(c) 1 and 3 only (d) 1, 2 and 3
Resignation of RBI Governor
Questions on Current Developments/ Initiatives
Prelims 2018
Which one of the following statements correctly describes the meaning of
legal tender money ?
(a) 	The money which is tendered in courts of law to defray the fee of legal
cases
(b) 	The money which a creditor is under compulsion to accept in settlement
of his claims
(c) 	The bank money in the form of cheques, drafts, bills of exchange, etc.
(d) 	The metallic money in circulation in a country
Demonetization in 2016
Prelims 2022
With reference to the Indian economy, what are the advantages of "Inflation-
Indexed Bonds (IIBs)"?
1. 	Government can reduce the coupon rates on its borrowing by way of IIBs.
2. 	IIBs 	provide 	protection 	to 	the 	investors 	from 	uncertainty 	regarding
inflation.
3. 	The interest received as well as capital gains on IIBs are not taxable.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only (b) 2 and 3 only (c) 1 and 3 only (d) 1, 2 and 3
Prelims 2019
Consider the following statements :
The 	RBI’s 	recent 	directives 	relating 	to 	'Storage 	of 	Payment 	System 	Data',
command the payment system providers that
1. they shall ensure that entire data relating to payment systems operated by
them are stored in a system only in India
2. they shall ensure that the systems are owned and operated by public sector
enterprises
3.they shall submit the consolidated system audit report to the CAG
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 only 	(b) 1 and 2 only (c) 3 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 3
Inflation Indexed Bonds 	RBI’s Data Localization Norms
Prelims 2023
With 	reference 	to 	Central 	Bank 	digital 	currencies, 	consider 	the 	following
statements:
1. 	It is possible to make payments in a digital currency without using the US
dollar or SWIFT system.
2. 	A digital currency can be distributed with a condition programmed into it
such as a time-frame for spending it.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 only 	(b) 2 only 	(c) Both 1 and 2 	(d) Neither 1 nor 2
CBDC Prelims 2024
Consider the following statements in respect of the digital rupee:
1. It is sovereign currency issued by the RBI in alignment with its monetary
policy.
2. It appears as a liability on the RBI's balance sheet.
3. It is insured against inflation by its very design.
4. It is freely convertible against commercial bank money and cash.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only 	(b) 1 and 3 only (c) 2 and 4 only (d) 1, 2 and 4
CBDC

By: Basava Uppin
List of Topics
1. Basics about RBI: Evolution, Structure, Objective.
2. Role of RBI
• 	Printing of Bank Notes
• 	Regulator of Banks
• 	Banker to Banks
• 	Banker to Government
• 	Debt Manager of the Government
• 	Regulator of Credit Information Companies
• 	Regulator of NBFCs
• 	Regulator of Authorized Dealers
• 	Management of Forex Reserves
3. Assets and Liabilities of RBI and Seignorage
4. Accounting Year of RBI
Static Part Current Developments
1. Payments Regulatory Board
2. Banking Laws Amendment Act, 2025
3. Recent Developments in CBDC
• 	Unified 	Market 	Interface 	(UMI): 	Tokenization 	of
Financial Assets
• 	Project M-Bridge and Agora
4. Floating Rate Savings Bonds
5. Sovereign Green Bonds and Greenium
6. STRIPS: Trading of State G-Secs
7. SIP in T-Bills on Retail Direct Platform
8. RBI’s Directives on E-Kuber Platform
9. RBI’s circular on Credit Information Companies
10. RBI’s Economic Capital Framework
11. Important Initiatives of RBI in news
• 	Mulehunter.AI
• 	Public Tech Platform for Frictionless credit
• 	Green Deposits Framework

Prelims 2021
Consider the following statements:
1. 	The Governor of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is appointed by the
Central Government.
2. 	Certain 	provisions 	in 	the 	Constitution 	of 	India 	give 	the 	Central
Government the right to issue directions to the RBI in public interest.
3. 	The Governor of the RBI draws his power from the RBI Act.
Which of the above statements are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only 	(b) 2 and 3 only 	(c) 1 and 3 only (d) 1, 2 and 3

Questions on Current Developments/ Initiatives
Prelims 2018
Which one of the following statements correctly describes the meaning of
legal tender money ?
(a) 	The money which is tendered in courts of law to defray the fee of legal
cases
(b) 	The money which a creditor is under compulsion to accept in settlement
of his claims
(c) 	The bank money in the form of cheques, drafts, bills of exchange, etc.
(d) 	The metallic money in circulation in a country
Demonetization in 2016
Prelims 2022
With reference to the Indian economy, what are the advantages of "Inflation-
Indexed Bonds (IIBs)"?
1. 	Government can reduce the coupon rates on its borrowing by way of IIBs.
2. 	IIBs 	provide 	protection 	to 	the 	investors 	from 	uncertainty 	regarding
inflation.
3. 	The interest received as well as capital gains on IIBs are not taxable.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only (b) 2 and 3 only (c) 1 and 3 only (d) 1, 2 and 3
Prelims 2019
Consider the following statements :
The 	RBI’s 	recent 	directives 	relating 	to 	'Storage 	of 	Payment 	System 	Data',
command the payment system providers that
1. they shall ensure that entire data relating to payment systems operated by
them are stored in a system only in India
2. they shall ensure that the systems are owned and operated by public sector
enterprises
3.they shall submit the consolidated system audit report to the CAG
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 only 	(b) 1 and 2 only (c) 3 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 3
Inflation Indexed Bonds 	RBI’s Data Localization Norms

Practice MCQ No.1
Which 	among 	the 	following 	best 	describes 	the 	concept 	of 	“Unclaimed
Deposits”, recently in news?
(a) 	Deposits that are not claimed by the depositors within 10 years and hence
become the property of banks.
(b) 	Deposits that lapse automatically after a 10 years of inactivity and become
the property of Government.
(c) 	Deposits in inactive bank accounts where withdrawals are prohibited
(d) 	Deposits that are not claimed by the depositors within 10 years, but the
legal ownership continues to vest with the depositor.
Practice MCQ No. 3
Consider the following statements:
Statement-I: The Banking Laws (Amendment) Act, 2025 permits depositors to
nominate up to 4 persons for bank deposits through simultaneous or successive
nomination, whereas in the case of bank lockers, only successive nomination is
permitted.
Statement-II: 	Simultaneous 	nomination 	for 	deposits 	enables 	depositors 	to
distribute 	the 	deposit 	amount 	among 	multiple 	nominees 	in 	predefined
proportions, thereby reducing disputes and delays in settlement.
Statement-III: Only successive nomination has been permitted for bank lockers
because the contents of lockers are not documented making simultaneous
nomination operationally complex.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
(a) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain
Statement I
(b) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains
Statement I
(c) Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
(d) Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct	Practice MCQ No 4

Board (PRB):

upon recommendations of Ratan Watal Committee.

3. 	The decisions of the PRB is based upon majority.
4. 	The Governor of the RBI enjoys veto power in the decisions of PRB.
Which among the statements given above is/are incorrect?
(a) 1, 2 and 4 only 	(b) 2 and 3 only 	(c) 3 and 4 only 	(d) 4 only

Consider the following statements:
Statement-I: The Banking Laws (Amendment) Act, 2025 permits depositors to
nominate up to 4 persons for bank deposits through simultaneous or successive
nomination, whereas in the case of bank lockers, only successive nomination is
permitted.
Statement-II: 	Simultaneous 	nomination 	for 	deposits 	enables 	depositors 	to
distribute 	the 	deposit 	amount 	among 	multiple 	nominees 	in 	predefined
proportions, thereby reducing disputes and delays in settlement.
Statement-III: Only successive nomination has been permitted for bank lockers
because the contents of lockers are not documented making simultaneous
nomination operationally complex.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
(a) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain
Statement I

3. 	CBDC 	can 	be 	transferred 	from 	one

Code
Offline and Programmable CBDC
CBDC can be programmed to be used only by
selected entities or for selected transactions.
Ex: 	CBDC 	can 	be 	used 	for 	transfer 	of 	Food
subsidy such that money can be used only for
buying food.
CBDC payments can be made without the need
for Internet.
1 	2 	3
4 	5 	6
7 	8 	9

By: Basava Uppin
Criteria 	Wholesale CBDC 	Retail CBDC
Users 	Selected Financial Institutions 	General Public, Non-Financial Consumers and Businesses.
Use 	Settlement of wholesale transactions.
Presently, it is limited to
(a) 	settlement of secondary market trade in G-secs
(b) 	Settlement of Inter bank borrowing and Lending in call
money
(c) 	Tokenised issuances and settlements of Certificate of
Deposits.
It can be used for Person-to-Person and Person-to-Merchant Payments.
Model of Issuance 	Direct 	Indirect
Denomination 	No Denomination 	Same Denomination as Coins and Notes
Form of CBDC 	Account based CBDC 	Token based CBDC
Degree of Anonymity 	Low 	High
Advantages 	Can make settlement system more efficient 	Promote cashless economy and Financial Inclusion
Practice MCQ No. 5
Consider the following statements related to Retail CBDC:
1. 	The Retail CBDC wallet can be provided by both banks and Non-Banks.
2. 	Presently, 	there 	is 	no 	need 	for 	Bank 	account 	for 	opening 	and
operating CBDC wallet.
3. 	Retail CBDC can be transferred to someone’s Bank Account.
How many statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 	Only One 	(b) Only Two 	(c) Only Three 	(d) All Four
Practice MCQ No. 6
With reference to Wholesale CBDC, consider the following statements:
1. 	The Wholesale CBDC is a token based CBDC.
2. 	The 	Wholesale 	CBDC 	can 	be 	used 	only 	by 	selected 	Financial
Institutions.
3. 	Presently, the wholesale CBDC can be used for making payments in the
primary market for buying G-Secs from RBI.
How many statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 	Only One (b) Only Two 	(c) Only Three 	(d) None

By: Basava Uppin
Prelims Pointers on Retail CBDC
Who can open CBDC wallet?: General Public, Non-Financial Consumers and Businesses.
Who can provide CBDC Wallet?: Selected Banks and Non-Bank.
Do we need Bank account for CBDC Wallet?: Yes. One needs to have Bank account to link it to CBDC Wallet ( RBI’s FAQ)
How can we load money into CBDC Wallet? : Through Bank Account or UPI Apps.
Is CBDC a legal Tender?: Yes
Can we transfer CBDC directly to someone’s Bank account?: No. It can be transferred only to CBDC wallet.
Can we redeem CBDC into Bank account? Yes
What are the denomination of CBDC? : CBDC is issued in the same denomination as paper currency and coins.
How to do transaction if we do not have required denomination or change? If you have to purchase a Rupee 15 item from a merchant, but
you have only Rupee 20 denomination available in your digital wallet. You can enter the Rupee15 in the amount field while sending the money,
remaining Rupee 5 will be stored automatically in your wallet.
Is there any minimum balance required to maintain in wallet?: No.
Will users be paid interest on CBDC? No.

question: Consider the following statements: Statement-I: The Unified Market Interface is a proposed digital platform developed by RBI for trading and settlement of tokenised financial market instruments using wholesale central bank digital currency. Statement-II: Settlement through wholesale CBDC in the Unified Market Interface enables delivery-versus-payment and hence addresses delays in settlement. Statement-III: The Unified Market Interface is intended to benefit retail investors who invest in financial instruments such as Bonds, G-Secs, Corporate Bonds etc. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

Consider the following statements:

Practice MCQ No. 8
The “Project M-Bridge”, sometime, seen in news, is
(a) 	Initiative of RBI to facilitate cross border instant payments
(b) 	Initiative of RBI to introduce interoperability between CBDC and UPI
(c) 	China-led Initiative to facilitate cross border payment through CBDC
(d) 	Initiative of Bank of International settlement (BIS) to facilitate 	cross
border payment through payment systems such as UPI.

By: Basava Uppin
Meaning: Multi-CBDC common platform for wholesale cross-border payments between commercial Banks of the countries. Initiative of Bank of
International Settlement (BIS) and is part of BIS Innovation Hub.
Founding Members: Hong Kong, China, Thailand and UAE. India is not part of this project.
Recent development (2025):
• 	BIS has exited from the Project mBridge. The project is now led by China wherein 95% of the transactions are carried out in Yuan.
• 	BIS has now taken up another project named as “Agora”.
Global M-CBDC Examples
Project Name 	Member countries
Project Aber 	Saudi Arabia and UAE
Project Dunbar 	Singapore, Malaysia,
Australia and South Africa
Project Jura 	Switzerland and France
Inthanon LionRock2 	Hong Kong and Thailand
Project Sela 	Hong Kong and Sela
Project Jasper 	Canada, UK and
Singapore
Reuters, Jan 2026

Practice MCQ No. 8
The “Project M-Bridge”, sometime, seen in news, is
(a) 	Initiative of RBI to facilitate cross border instant payments
(b) 	Initiative of RBI to introduce interoperability between CBDC and UPI
(c) 	China-led Initiative to facilitate cross border payment through CBDC
(d) 	Initiative of Bank of International settlement (BIS) to facilitate 	cross
border payment through payment systems such as UPI.

By: Basava Uppin
Meaning: Multi-CBDC common platform for wholesale cross-border payments between commercial Banks of the countries. Initiative of Bank of
International Settlement (BIS) and is part of BIS Innovation Hub.
Founding Members: Hong Kong, China, Thailand and UAE. India is not part of this project.
Recent development (2025):
• 	BIS has exited from the Project mBridge. The project is now led by China wherein 95% of the transactions are carried out in Yuan.
• 	BIS has now taken up another project named as “Agora”.
Global M-CBDC Examples
Project Name 	Member countries
Project Aber 	Saudi Arabia and UAE
Project Dunbar 	Singapore, Malaysia,
Australia and South Africa
Project Jura 	Switzerland and France
Inthanon LionRock2 	Hong Kong and Thailand
Project Sela 	Hong Kong and Sela
Project Jasper 	Canada, UK and
Singapore
Reuters, Jan 2026

By: Basava Uppin
E-Kuber Platform (2012)
Meaning: Core Banking Solution Platform of RBI.
Participants: Banks, Primary Dealers, Insurance Companies, Mutual
Fund Companies etc.
Purpose:
• 	Maintain Current Account with the RBI
• 	Enable Banks to Maintain CRR Balances
• 	Inter-Bank Settlements
• 	Provide Ways and Means advances to Government.
• 	Purchase of G-Secs from the RBI in Primary Market.
Note: Retail Investors are not participants on the E-Kuber Platform.
Hence, they cannot purchase G-Secs directly from the RBI in the
Primary Market through E-Kuber Platform.
Recent 	Development 	(July 	2025): 	All 	the 	payments 	made 	by
Government ministries and Departments above Rs 75 crores must be
made through E-Kuber Platform.
Retail Direct Platform (2021)
Purpose: Enable Retail Investors to purchase G-Secs from the RBI in the Primary
Market.
Requirement: Retail Investors need to open Retail Direct Gilt Account with the RBI.
G-Secs 	which 	can 	be 	bought?: 	Dated 	Secs, 	SDLs, 	T-Bills 	Sovereign 	Gold 	Bonds,
Sovereign Green Bonds and Floating Rate Savings Bonds.
Who can open account?: Only Individuals- Both Residents and Non-Residents.
Facilities provided on Portal:
• 	Buying G-Secs in the Primary Market
• 	Buying and Selling G-Secs in the Secondary Market
Minimum and Maximum Investment Limit:

By: Basava Uppin
E-Kuber Platform (2012)
Meaning: Core Banking Solution Platform of RBI.
Participants: Banks, Primary Dealers, Insurance Companies, Mutual
Fund Companies etc.
Purpose:
• 	Maintain Current Account with the RBI
• 	Enable Banks to Maintain CRR Balances
• 	Inter-Bank Settlements
• 	Provide Ways and Means advances to Government.
• 	Purchase of G-Secs from the RBI in Primary Market.
Note: Retail Investors are not participants on the E-Kuber Platform.
Hence, they cannot purchase G-Secs directly from the RBI in the
Primary Market through E-Kuber Platform.
Recent 	Development 	(July 	2025): 	All 	the 	payments 	made 	by
Government ministries and Departments above Rs 75 crores must be
made through E-Kuber Platform.
Retail Direct Platform (2021)
Purpose: Enable Retail Investors to purchase G-Secs from the RBI in the Primary
Market.
Requirement: Retail Investors need to open Retail Direct Gilt Account with the RBI.
G-Secs 	which 	can 	be 	bought?: 	Dated 	Secs, 	SDLs, 	T-Bills 	Sovereign 	Gold 	Bonds,
Sovereign Green Bonds and Floating Rate Savings Bonds.
Who can open account?: Only Individuals- Both Residents and Non-Residents.
Facilities provided on Portal:
• 	Buying G-Secs in the Primary Market
• 	Buying and Selling G-Secs in the Secondary Market
Minimum and Maximum Investment Limit:
• 	Minimum Limit: Rs 10,000 (Dated Secs, SDLs and T-Bills) and 1 Gm (Sovereign Gold
Bonds)
• 	Maximum Limit: Different for different Instruments (not important for exam)
Recent Development (August 2025): RBI has enabled SIP in T-Bills on the Retail Direct
Platform. (Auto-bidding Facility for Investment and Re-Investment)
Important Platforms of the RBI
Negotiated Dealing System-Order Matching (NDS-OM)
Meaning: Platform for Trading of G-Secs in the Secondary Market.
Owned by: RBI
Operated by: Clearing Corporation of India Limited.

RBI’s guidelines on Credit Information Companies (CICs)
Membership of CICs: All Credit providers including ARCs must become members
of all CICs.
Reporting of data by Credit providers to all CICs on fortnightly basis. To be shifted
to weekly basis from April 1, 2026.
Access to data: Individuals can access their credit report from the CICs free of
cost only once in a year.
Customer Alerts: CICs must now alert customers via SMS or email whenever their
Credit report is accessed
Grievance 	Redressal 	and 	Compensation: 	Complainants 	are 	entitled 	to
compensation of Rs 100 per calendar day if their complaint regarding credit
information inaccuracies is not resolved within thirty calendar days.
Business Standard, Jan 2025

subject: Economy
question: In line with the recommendations of Bimal Jalan Committee on RBI’s Economic Capital Framework, which among the following can be considered as sources of Realized profits of RBI and hence transferrable to the Government? 1. Interest on loans given by RBI to Banks 2. Appreciation in Foreign Currency with RBI 3. Appreciation in value of Gold in custody of RBI 4. Interest on G-Secs in custody of RBI Select the correct answer using the code given below:
context:
By: Basava Uppin
Business line, May 2025 	Money control, May 2025
Earlier Mechanism (Before 2019)
RBI’s assets 	Earns Profits 	Meets its expenses 	Net Profits
Transfer as Dividend to
Govt.
Transfer to RBI’s
Reserves
Dispute between RBI and
Centre
• 	No 	Objective 	criteria 	on
Transfer of Dividend
• 	No 	Objective 	Criteria 	on
Adequacy of Buffer
Economic Capital Framework
RBI’s assets 	Earns Profits 	Meets its expenses 	Net Profits Is Contingency Risk
Buffer equal to the
threshold?
Yes Transfer entire Net
Profits as Dividend to
Govt.
No
Transfer the Net
profits so that the
Buffer increases to
threshold level. Then
Transfer the remaining
money as Dividend to
Govt.
Students’ doubt: Are both Realized Profits and Unrealized Profits transferrable to Govt?
Basava’s Response: No. Only the Realized Profits are transferrable to Government.
Note: Earlier, the Contingency Risk Buffer was in the range of 5.5% to 6.5% of the RBI’s Assets. However, under the revised framework, the CRB has been
fixed in the range of 6.0 ± 1.5% of total assets. The RBI has set the CRB at 7.5% for the year 2025-26.

By: Basava Uppin
RBI’s Economic Capital Framework
Meaning: 	Deals 	with 	adequacy 	of 	RBI’s 	Reserves 	and 	Transfer 	of
Dividend to the Government. Laid down by Bimal Jalan Committee.
Legal Framework: RBI is not required to pay tax on its profits. Section
47 of RBI Act, 1934 mandates that profits earned by RBI should be
transferred to Government.
Sources of realised Profits for RBI:
• 	Interest earned on Foreign Currency Assets
• 	Interest earned on loans given to Government and Banks
Sources of Unrealised Profits of RBI: Appreciation in value of Gold and
FCAs
Reasons for record profits of RBI: Sale of Dollars to control Rupee
Depreciation
Practice MCQ No. 17
In line with the recommendations of Bimal Jalan Committee on RBI’s Economic
Capital Framework, which among the following can be considered as sources
of Realized profits of RBI and hence transferrable to the Government?
1. Interest on loans given by RBI to Banks
2. Appreciation in Foreign Currency with RBI
3. Appreciation in value of Gold in custody of RBI
4. Interest on G-Secs in custody of RBI
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
(a) 1 and 2 only 	(b) 2 and 3 only 	(c) 1 and 4 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 4 only

Consider the following statements:
Statement-I: 	The RBI has transferred record surplus of more than Rs 2 lakh
crores as dividend to the Government in 2025.
Statement-II: 	The 	RBI 	has 	reviewed 	its 	economic 	capital 	framework 	and
reduced the threshold for the Contingency risk buffer in 2025.
Statement-III: The RBI has made profits by selling dollars to control Rupee
Depreciation.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
(a) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain
Statement I
(b) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them
explains Statement I
(c) Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
(d) Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

By: Basava Uppin
Prelims Pointers on Deposit Insurance
Coverage of Banks 	Commercial banks, Regional rural banks, Local area banks (LABs),
Payment Banks, Small Finance Banks, and Cooperative banks.
Note: NBFCs and Rural Cooperative Societies are not covered under
Deposit insurance.
Deposits Covered 	Savings, Fixed, Current and Recurring Deposits.
Deposits not
Covered
Deposits 	of 	Foreign 	Governments, 	Central/State 	Governments;
Inter-Bank Deposits.
Premium 	Paid by the banks and hence the cost is not directly borne by the
deposit holder.
Deposit Insurance 	Rs 5 lakhs
Deposit Insurance
and Credit
Guarantee
Corporation
(Amendment) Act
2021
Timeline for payment: Earlier, Deposit Insurance was available
upon 	liquidation, 	reconstruction 	or 	merger 	of 	Bank. 	New
Amendment enables the depositors to withdraw up to Rs 5 lakh
within 90 days upon placing a Bank under moratorium.
Premium paid by banks to the DICGC: The Act limits the rate of
premium (per annum) for a bank at 0.15% of its total outstanding
deposits. 	The act has now allowed the DICGC to increase this
maximum limit with the prior approval of RBI.
Repayment by the bank to the Corporation: Under the Act, once
the DICGC makes payment to the depositors, the insured bank
becomes liable to repay the same amount to the Corporation. The
new amendment provides that 	the Corporation may provide for
the time limit for repayment by the Bank.
Risk based
Insurance Premium

Prelims Pointers on Deposit Insurance
Coverage of Banks 	Commercial banks, Regional rural banks, Local area banks (LABs),
Payment Banks, Small Finance Banks, and Cooperative banks.
Note: NBFCs and Rural Cooperative Societies are not covered under
Deposit insurance.
Deposits Covered 	Savings, Fixed, Current and Recurring Deposits.
Deposits not
Covered
Deposits 	of 	Foreign 	Governments, 	Central/State 	Governments;
Inter-Bank Deposits.
Premium 	Paid by the banks and hence the cost is not directly borne by the
deposit holder.
Deposit Insurance 	Rs 5 lakhs
Deposit Insurance
and Credit
Guarantee
Corporation
(Amendment) Act
2021
Timeline for payment: Earlier, Deposit Insurance was available
upon 	liquidation, 	reconstruction 	or 	merger 	of 	Bank. 	New
Amendment enables the depositors to withdraw up to Rs 5 lakh
within 90 days upon placing a Bank under moratorium.
Premium paid by banks to the DICGC: The Act limits the rate of
premium (per annum) for a bank at 0.15% of its total outstanding
deposits. 	The act has now allowed the DICGC to increase this
maximum limit with the prior approval of RBI.
Repayment by the bank to the Corporation: Under the Act, once
the DICGC makes payment to the depositors, the insured bank
becomes liable to repay the same amount to the Corporation. The
new amendment provides that 	the Corporation may provide for
the time limit for repayment by the Bank.

Insurance Premium

By: Basava Uppin
Framework for acceptance of Green Deposits
Applicability: Scheduled Commercial Banks, NBFCs and Housing Finance
Companies (HFCs)
Allocation of Funds:
• 	Renewable Energy
• 	Energy Efficiency
• 	Clean Transportation
• 	Climate Change Adaptation
• 	Pollution Control
• 	Sustainable management of natural resources and waste management.
Can the green deposits be denominated in foreign currency? No. It should
be in the form of Indian Rupees only.
Important Initiatives of RBI
Public Tech Platform for Frictionless Credit/ Unified Lending Interface (ULI)
Objective: To enable frictionless credit delivery by facilitating seamless flow of required
digital information to lenders.
Key 	Feature: 	End-to-end 	digital 	platform 	with 	open 	architecture, 	open 	Application
Programming Interfaces (APIs), and standards for easy integration ("plug and play" model).
Data Sources: Aims to integrate data from various entities like:
• 	Central and State governments (e.g., land records)
• 	Account Aggregators (AAs)
• 	Banks
• 	Credit Information Companies
• 	Digital Identity Authorities (e.g., Aadhaar e-KYC, e-signing)
• 	PAN Validation
Is it a lending Platform?: The Platform is not a lending platform. It only helps the Banks to
access data.
Developed by: Reserve Bank Innovation Hub (RBIH), a wholly-owned subsidiary of the RBI.
Note: Open Credit Enablement Network (OCEN):-Open API developed by iSPIRT (Indian
Software Product Industry Round Table), a non-profit technology think tank to democratise
credit.
Depositor Education and Awareness (DEA) Fund Scheme
Background: Formulated under Banking Regulation act, 1949 to educate
Depositors about Banking services and their rights.
Depositor Education and Awareness Fund is funded from the unclaimed
deposits with the Banks.

Definition: Repo rate is the rate at which Banks borrow money
from the RBI.
Example: Duration: 14 Days; Repo rate: 4%.
Duration: 1 day, 7-day, 14-day, 28-day, 56 day etc.
Rate of Interest is decided through Auction.
Example:
• 	RBI issues notification for 14-day Repo in which it highlights that it
is willing to lend Rs 100 Crores.
• 	All the Banks will be required to place their bid in terms of Rate of
Interest on E-Kuber Platform.
• 	Bid rate cannot be equal to or less than Repo Rate
• 	Bids by Banks: Bank “X” (5.3%); Bank “Y” (5.4%); Bank ”Z” (5.5%)
• 	Repo (Loan) would be allocated to highest Bidder i.e., Bank Z
Hence, RBI does not lend loans below Repo Rate and hence it is also
called as Benchmark.

What does the RBI do? 	RBI Sells dollars with an agreement to buy back same
amount of dollars at future date and at fixed exchange rate
RBI Buys dollars with an agreementt to sell amount
of dollars at future date and at fixed exchange rate
When is it adopted? 	Shortage of dollarsà Rupee Depreciation.
Hence, RBI sells dollars and sucks out Rupee to
control Rupee Depreciation
What does it lead to? 	Rupee Revaluation: Increase in value of Rupee due to
RBI’s Intervention
Rupee Devaluation: Decrease in value of Rupee due
to RBI’s Intervention
Impact on Rupee Liquidity 	Decreases 	Increases
Impact on Rate of
Interest on Loans Decrease in Rupee Liquidityà Increase in Rate of
Interest on Loans
Control Rupee Appreciation + Promote Growth
through Expansionary Policy
Control Rupee Depreciation + Control Inflation
through Contractionary Policy
Dual Objectives fulfilled
Impact on Forex Reserves 	RBI sells dollarsà Decline in Forex Reserves 	RBI Purchases dollarsà Increase in Forex Reserves
Surplus of dollarsà Rupee Appreciation
Hence, RBI buys dollars and injects Rupee to control
Rupee Appreciation
Increase in Rupee Liquidityà Decrease in Rate of
Interest on Loans

question: Which among the following measures is/are likely to be taken to check Rupee Depreciation? 1. Curb import of non-essential items 2. Encourage issuance of Masala Bonds 3. Adopt Forex Sell/Buy Swap 4. Adopt Forex Buy/Sell Swap Select the correct answer using the code given below:
context:
By: Basava Uppin	Criteria 	Forex Sell/Buy Swap 	Forex Buy/Sell Swap
What does the RBI do? 	RBI Sells dollars with an agreement to buy back same
amount of dollars at future date and at fixed exchange rate
RBI Buys dollars with an agreementt to sell amount
of dollars at future date and at fixed exchange rate
When is it adopted? 	Shortage of dollarsà Rupee Depreciation.
Hence, RBI sells dollars and sucks out Rupee to
control Rupee Depreciation
What does it lead to? 	Rupee Revaluation: Increase in value of Rupee due to
RBI’s Intervention
Rupee Devaluation: Decrease in value of Rupee due
to RBI’s Intervention
Impact on Rupee Liquidity 	Decreases 	Increases
Impact on Rate of
Interest on Loans Decrease in Rupee Liquidityà Increase in Rate of
Interest on Loans
Control Rupee Appreciation + Promote Growth
through Expansionary Policy
Control Rupee Depreciation + Control Inflation
through Contractionary Policy
Dual Objectives fulfilled
Impact on Forex Reserves 	RBI sells dollarsà Decline in Forex Reserves 	RBI Purchases dollarsà Increase in Forex Reserves
Surplus of dollarsà Rupee Appreciation
Hence, RBI buys dollars and injects Rupee to control
Rupee Appreciation
Increase in Rupee Liquidityà Decrease in Rate of
Interest on Loans

Prelims 2015
When the Reserve Bank of India reduces the Statutory Liquidity Ratio by 50
basis points which of the following is likely to happen?
(a) 	India's GDP growth rate increases drastically.
(b) 	Foreign 	Institutional 	Investors 	may 	bring 	more 	capital 	into 	our
country.
(c) 	Scheduled Commercial Banks may cut their lending rates.
(d) 	It may drastically reduce the liquidity to the banking system.
Practice MCQ No.31
Consider the following scenario:
The banks in India are taking more loans from the RBI and depositing less
money with the RBI under Reverse Repo window.
What could be the cause for such a scenario?
(a) 	Surplus in the Banking Liquidity
(b) 	Decrease in Credit Creation by Banks
(c) 	Decrease in Demand for Currency by Public
(d) 	Deficit in the Banking Liquidity
Practice MCQ No.32
Which among the following is likely to decrease Banking System Liquidity in
Indian Economy?
1. 	Increase in Demand for Currency
2. 	Decrease in Credit Deposit Ratio
3. 	RBI’s Intervention to control Rupee Depreciation.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
(a) 	1 and 2 only 	(b) 2 only 	(c) 1 and 3 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 3
Practice MCQ No. 33
Consider the following statements:
Statement-I: 	In the recent past, the Banking system liquidity in the Indian Economy has
turned into deficit.
Statement-II: The Credit-Deposit Ratio of the Indian Banks has increased in the recent
past.
Statement-III: Th decrease in Banking system liquidity leads to increase in the rate of
Interest on loans.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
(a) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
(b) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains
Statement I
(c) Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
(d) Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct
Practice MCQ No. 34
Consider the following statements:
Statement-I: 	On a given day, if Banks take more loans from RBI and deposit less money
with RBI, then it means that Banking system liquidity is in deficit.
Statement-II: On a given day, if Banks take more loans from RBI and deposit less money
with RBI, then it leads to increase in Banking System Liquidity.
A. 	Both 	Statement-I 	and 	Statement-II 	are 	correct 	and 	Statement-II 	is 	the 	correct
explanation for Statement-I.
B. 	Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is not the correct
explanation for Statement-I.
C. 	Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
D. 	Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct.

Jan 2026
Accepts Deposits
Maintains CRR and SLR 	Loans
Credit-Deposit Ratio (Loan-to-Deposit Ratio)
Loans given by the Banks/ Deposits with Banks)
*100
Interpretation: Indicates how much money mobilized through Deposits has been given in the
form of loans.
Higher Credit-Deposit Ratio
Reasons for increase in Credit
• 	Increase in market borrowings by Banks
• 	Increase 	in 	share 	of 	equity 	capital 	in
funding loans
• 	Loans from Reserve Bank of India
Reasons for decrease in Deposits
• 	Increase in household debt
• 	Lower Interest rates on Deposits
• 	Shift towards Small Savings Schemes, Mutual
Funds, Shares etc.
Problems with higher Credit-Deposit ratio
• 	Liquidity risk
• 	Credit risk
• 	Higher cost of Funds as there is pressure on the Banks to mobilize more deposits by
increasing interest rates.
• 	Decline in Net Interest Margin

• 	Increase in market borrowings by Banks
• 	Increase 	in 	share 	of 	equity 	capital 	in
funding loans
• 	Loans from Reserve Bank of India
Reasons for decrease in Deposits
• 	Increase in household debt
• 	Lower Interest rates on Deposits
• 	Shift towards Small Savings Schemes, Mutual
Funds, Shares etc.
Problems with higher Credit-Deposit ratio
• 	Liquidity risk
• 	Credit risk
• 	Higher cost of Funds as there is pressure on the Banks to mobilize more deposits by
increasing interest rates.
• 	Decline in Net Interest Margin

Basava’s response: No. However, RBI uses CD Ratio to monitor

Total: Rs 80

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following pairs: Ratio Meaning 1. Currency Deposit ratio Ratio of Currency with Public to Deposits with Banks 2. Cash Deposit ratio Ratio of Cash in hand with Banks and CRR Balances with RBI to Deposits 3. Investment Deposit Ratio Ratio of Loans to total Deposits Which among the above pairs is/are correctly matched?
context:
By: Basava Uppin
Important Differences
Criteria 	Cash Deposit ratio 	Currency Deposit Ratio 	Credit Deposit Ratio 	Investment Deposit Ratio
Meaning 	Ratio of cash in hand and balances with
the 	RBI 	as 	percentage 	of 	aggregate
deposits.
Ratio of money held by the Public in the
form of currency to Deposits with the
Banks.
Ratio of Credit to the Deposits with
the Banks.
Interpretation:
Ratio of Investments by Banks in the
G-Secs to the total deposits.
Formula 	((Cash in Hand + CRR Balances)/ Total
Deposits)*100
(Currency with the Public/Total
Deposits) * 100
(Loans given by the Banks/ Deposits
with Banks) *100
(Investment in G-Secs/ Deposits with
Banks) *100
Interpretation 	Indicates 	how 	much 	cash 	banks
maintain for each rupee of deposit they
accept.
Indicates how much cash people
maintain with themselves for each
rupee of deposits.
Indicates how much money mobilized
through Deposits has been given in
the form of loans.
Indicates how much deposits have
been invested in G-Secs.
Example 	If the Cash Deposit ratio is 5%, it means
that Banks have around Rs 5 in form of
cash against deposits of around Rs 100.
If the Currency Deposit Ratio is 15%, it
means that Public hold Rs 15 in the form
of cash for every Rs 100 worth Deposits.
if the Credit Deposit ratio is 75%, then
it means 75% of Deposits has been
used to give loans.

subject: Economy
question: What is/are the purpose/purposes of the `Marginal Cost of Funds based Lending Rate (MCLR)’ announced by RBI? 1. These guidelines help improve the transparency in the methodology followed by banks for determining the interest rates on advances. 2. These guidelines help ensure availability of bank credit at interest rates which are fair to the borrowers as well as the banks. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
context:
By: Basava Uppin
External Benchmarking of Loans
Applicability? 	Floating rate Personal loans, retail loans (home, auto), MSME loans
Can it be made applicable to
other category of loans?
Yes. Banks can extend the regime to other borrowers as well.
Rate of Interest linked to? 	Any of the 4 External Benchmarks:
ü 	Repo Rate
ü 	Yield rate on T-91
ü 	Yield Rate on T-182
ü 	Any other benchmark published by FBIL
Interest Rate resets 	ü 	Once every 3 months
Spread 	Banks have the discretion to fix their own spread. But once the spread is fixed, Banks cannot change it.
RBI’s rules on Floating rate
Loans
Applicability: Personal Loans ( Consumer Credit, Credit Card, Home Loans, Vehicle Loans, Education Loans, Loans given for
investment in shares and Bonds etc.)
Option to Switch: In case of Increase in Repo rate, borrowers have the option to switch from Floating rate loan to Fixed rate
loan. Similarly, in case of decrease in Repo rate, borrowers have option to switch from fixed rate loan to Floating rate loan.
However, the Bank can specify the number of times the switch option can be exercised by the borrower.
Option to borrowers: Borrowers shall also be given the choice to opt for (i) enhancement in EMI or elongation of tenor or for a
combination of both options; and, (ii) to prepay, either in part or in full, at any point during the tenor of the loan.
Economic Times, Jan 2025 	Dec 2025

By: Basava Uppin	Monetary Policy Transmission
Factors affecting Monetary Policy Transmission (MPT) in India
External Benchmarking of Loans 	Most of the Banks have linked Floating rate loans to the Repo Rate under the EBL regime. Hence, as the Repo rate changes, rate of
Interest on Loans also change.
Banking System Liquidity 	Meaning: Readily available cash that banks need to meet short-term business and financial needs.
During Inflationà Banking system liquidity should be lowerà Force Banks to increase deposit and lending rates.
During Slowdownà Banking system liquidity should be higherà Force Banks to reduce deposit and lending rate.
Higher Dependence on Deposits
as compared to RBI
Scenario 1: Bank ”X” is dependent more on RBI to give loans to public. As soon as RBI increases or decreases repo rate, Bank would
increase or decrease its rate of interest on loans.
Scenario 2: Bank “Y” is dependent more on deposits to give loans to public. Even th

Applicability? 	Floating rate Personal loans, retail loans (home, auto), MSME loans
Can it be made applicable to
other category of loans?
Yes. Banks can extend the regime to other borrowers as well.
Rate of Interest linked to? 	Any of the 4 External Benchmarks:
ü 	Repo Rate
ü 	Yield rate on T-91
ü 	Yield Rate on T-182
ü 	Any other benchmark published by FBIL
Interest Rate resets 	ü 	Once every 3 months
Spread 	Banks have the discretion to fix their own spread. But once the spread is fixed, Banks cannot change it.
RBI’s rules on Floating rate
Loans
Applicability: Personal Loans ( Consumer Credit, Credit Card, Home Loans, Vehicle Loans, Education Loans, Loans given for
investment in shares and Bonds etc.)
Option to Switch: In case of Increase in Repo rate, borrowers have the option to switch from Floating rate loan to Fixed rate
loan. Similarly, in case of decrease in Repo rate, borrowers have option to switch from fixed rate loan to Floating rate loan.
However, the Bank can specify the number of times the switch option can be exercised by the borrower.
Option to borrowers: Borrowers shall also be given the choice to opt for (i) enhancement in EMI or elongation of tenor or for a
combination of both options; and, (ii) to prepay, either in part or in full, at any point during the tenor of the loan.
Economic Times, Jan 2025 	Dec 2025

subject: Economy
question: Indian Government Bond Yields are influenced by which of the following? 1. Actions of the United States Federal Reserve 2. Actions of the Reserve Bank of India 3. Inflation and short-term interest rates Select the correct answer using the code given below.
context:
By: Basava Uppin	Monetary Policy Transmission
Factors affecting Monetary Policy Transmission (MPT) in India
External Benchmarking of Loans 	Most of the Banks have linked Floating rate loans to the Repo Rate under the EBL regime. Hence, as the Repo rate changes, rate of
Interest on Loans also change.
Banking System Liquidity 	Meaning: Readily available cash that banks need to meet short-term business and financial needs.
During Inflationà Banking system liquidity should be lowerà Force Banks to increase deposit and lending rates.
During Slowdownà Banking system liquidity should be higherà Force Banks to reduce deposit and lending rate.
Higher Dependence on Deposits
as compared to RBI
Scenario 1: Bank ”X” is dependent more on RBI to give loans to public. As soon as RBI increases or decreases repo rate, Bank would
increase or decrease its rate of interest on loans.
Scenario 2: Bank “Y” is dependent more on deposits to give loans to public. Even though RBI increases or decreases repo rate, Bank may
not change its interest rate on deposits.
Higher Capital Adequacy Ratio
(CAR)
When the Repo rate is reduced, Bank with higher CAR may be able to provide loans at the lower rate of interest
Gross NPAs 	When the Repo rate is reduced, Bank with higher NPA may find it difficult be to provide loans at the lower rate of interest
Credit-Deposit Ratio 	Credit Deposit ratio denotes how much money mobilized through Deposits has been given in the form of loans. For example, if the ratio
is 75%, then it means 75% of Deposits has been used to give loans.
Higher Credit Deposit ratio strengthens MPT as changes in Repo and Reverse Repo would have effect on Deposit rates and lending rates.

Sustained Deflation in Japanese Economy
Central Bank adopted Negative Interest Rate Policy
Yield Curve Control: Yield rate on 10-year Bonds
maintained around 0%.
Objective:
Discourage Banks from depositing 	surplus money
with Central Bank.
Discourage people from buying long term Bonds
Japan’s Economy between 1998 to 2022
Implications:
• 	Carry Trade: Investors borrowed at cheaper rate
of Interest in Japan and invested in US and Indian
Bonds
• 	Japan became the world’s largest creditor Nation
($ 3.7 trillion)
Increase in Inflation due to Weaker Yen, Increase in global energy
prices and Food Prices (Cost-Push Inflation)
Central Bank gave up Negative Interest rate Policy and Yield Curve
Control
Increase in Bond Yields in Japanese Economy
Japan’s Economy between 2022 to present
Implications for Japan
• 	Increase in borrowing cost
• 	Hurt financial sector which has
invested in Japanese Bonds due
to fall in their prices.
Implications for Global Economy
• 	Decrease in foreign inflows into
US and Indian Bonds
• 	Germany has overtaken Japan
as the largest Creditor Nation.

question: Consider the following statements: Statement-I: Germany has replaced Japan as the world’s largest creditor nation after more than 3 decades. Statement-II: There has been increase in Bond Yields in Japan leading to large scale foreign outflows from Japan to other countries. Which one of the following is correct in respect of above statements?

options.a: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct explanation for Statement-I.
options.b: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is not the correct explanation for Statement-I.
options.c: Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
options.d: Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct.

context:
By: Basava Uppin
CNBC, May 2025
Sustained Deflation in Japanese Economy
Central Bank adopted Negative Interest Rate Policy
Yield Curve Control: Yield rate on 10-year Bonds
maintained around 0%.
Objective:
Discourage Banks from depositing 	surplus money
with Central Bank.
Discourage people from buying long term Bonds
Japan’s Economy between 1998 to 2022
Implications:
• 	Carry Trade: Investors borrowed at cheaper rate
of Interest in Japan and invested in US and Indian
Bonds
• 	Japan became the world’s largest creditor Nation
($ 3.7 trillion)
Increase in Inflation due to Weaker Yen, Increase in global energy
prices and Food Prices (Cost-Push Inflation)
Central Bank gave up Negative Interest rate Policy and Yield Curve
Control
Increase in Bond Yields in Japanese Economy
Japan’s Economy between 2022 to present
Implications for Japan
• 	Increase in borrowing cost
• 	Hurt financial sector which has
invested in Japanese Bonds due
to fall in their prices.
Implications for Global Economy
• 	Decrease in foreign inflows into
US and Indian Bonds
• 	Germany has overtaken Japan
as the largest Creditor Nation.
Times of India, May 2025

By: Basava Uppin
Practice MCQ N0. 47
Consider the following statements:
Statement-I: Recently, the spread between US Government bond yields and
Indian Bond Yields has reduced to 20-year low.
Statement-II: 	The Bond 	Yields in 	USA 	has increased 	due to 	downgrade in
Sovereign credit ratings while Bond Yields in India has decreased due to strong
macro-economic fundamentals.
Statement-III: The decrease in spread between US Government Bond yields and
Indian Bond Yields generally leads to outflow of FPI from India.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
(a) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain
Statement I
(b) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains
Statement I
(c) Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
(d) Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

question: Which among the following best describes the concept of ”Universal Banking”?

options.a: Banks having physical presence across country.

options.d: Banks providing loans to corporates, public and

context:
By: Basava Uppin	RBI’s Guidelines for On-Tap Licensing of Universal Banks
Eligible Promoters 	• 	Existing NBFCs with successful track record of 10 years
• 	Individuals having 10 years experience in Banking and Finance
• 	Resident Indian Companies ( If total assets is more than Rs 5000 crores,
then non-financial assets should be less than 40%)
• 	Small Finance Banks which fulfill the criteria
Minimum Capital 	• 	Rs 1000 crores (Earlier, it was Rs 500 crores). Bank must get listed on
stock exchanges within 6 years.
Corporate Structure 	Banks to be set up through Non-Operative Financial Holding Company
(NOFHC). NOFHC to be registered as NBFC.
Is it mandatory to set up Bank
only through NOFHC?
If the promoter is individual or stand-alone entity that do not have other
entities, then there is no need to set up NOFHC.
Are Large Industrial Houses
allowed to open Banks?
No. But they are allowed to invest in Banks of up to 10%.
Activities which can be
undertaken by Bank
Banks 	can 	undertake 	Insurance, 	mutual 	funds, 	stock 	broking 	functions
through separate Subsidiary / Joint Venture. Can also give loans for IPOs.
Lending Loans 	Can Lend loans against collateral such as Shares, Bonds, Mutual Funds etc.
Pattern of Shareholding 	NOFHC shall hold a minimum of 40 per cent of the paid-up voting equity
capital of the bank which shall be locked-in for a period of five years. The
promoter group shareholding shall be brought down to 26 per cent (Earlier
15%) within a period of 15 years from the date of commencement of
business of the bank.
Is FDI Allowed? 	Yes. Up to 74%.
Branch Norms 	At least 25% of the Branches should be opened in Unbanked areas.
Latest Developments (2025) 	• 	Standing External Advisory Committee (SEAC): Applications for Universal
Banks to be initially screened by the RBI and evaluated by SEAC.
• 	RBI gave license to AU Small Finance Bank to transition into Universal
Bank
Quint, July 2025
Business line, June 2025
Business Standard, Jan 2025
ET, Aug 2025
Mint, Dec 2025

subject: Economy
question: With respect to “On-Tap Licensing” guidelines for Private Banks, consider the following statements: 1. The Banks must be set up only through Non-Operative Financial Holding Company (NOFHC) in India. 2. Existing NBFCs are not allowed to apply for Banking License 3. FDI is not allowed in the Banks in India. Which among the statements given above is/are incorrect?
context:
By: Basava Uppin	RBI’s Guidelines for On-Tap Licensing of Universal Banks
Eligible Promoters 	• 	Existing NBFCs with successful track record of 10 years
• 	Individuals having 10 years experience in Banking and Finance
• 	Resident Indian Companies ( If total assets is more than Rs 5000 crores,
then non-financial assets should be less than 40%)
• 	Small Finance Banks which fulfill the criteria
Minimum Capital 	• 	Rs 1000 crores (Earlier, it was Rs 500 crores). Bank must get listed on
stock exchanges within 6 years.
Corporate Structure 	Banks to be set up through Non-Operative Financial Holding Company
(NOFHC). NOFHC to be registered as NBFC.
Is it mandatory to set up Bank
only through NOFHC?
If the promoter is individual or stand-alone entity that do not have other
entities, then there is no need to set up NOFHC.
Are Large Industrial Houses
allowed to open Banks?
No. But they are allowed to invest in Banks of up to 10%.
Activities which can be
undertaken by Bank
Banks 	can 	undertake 	Insurance, 	mutual 	funds, 	stock 	broking 	functions
through separate Subsidiary / Joint Venture. Can also give loans for IPOs.
Lending Loans 	Can Lend loans against collateral such as Shares, Bonds, Mutual Funds etc.
Pattern of Shareholding 	NOFHC shall hold a minimum of 40 per cent of the paid-up voting equity
capital of the bank which shall be locked-in for a period of five years. The
promoter group shareholding shall be brought down to 26 per cent (Earlier
15%) within a period of 15 years from the date of commencement of
business of the bank.
Is FDI Allowed? 	Yes. Up to 74%.
Branch Norms 	At least 25% of the Branches should be opened in Unbanked areas.
Latest Developments (2025) 	• 	Standing External Advisory Committee (SEAC): Applications for Universal
Banks to be initially screened by the RBI and evaluated by SEAC.
• 	RBI gave license to AU Small Finance Bank to transition into Universal
Bank

RBI
Monitor Financial Health
of Banks and NBFCs using
parameters such as NPA,

Sets Benchmark for
Parameters. For example:
NPA of more than 6%
If the Benchmark crosses
threshold (For example,
NPA of Bank “X” is more
than 6%)
RBI places the Bank
“X” under PCA
Framework
RBI imposes restrictions
on Bank “X” to improve
its financial health
Restrictions include:
• 	Prevent opening of more branches
• 	Undertake special audit
• 	Restricting Dividend Payment
• 	Supersede Bank’s Board (in case of
Private Banks)
If the Financial Position of
Bank “X” improves, the
Bank is removed from
PCA Framework
Hence, idea of PCA is to
take prompt action and
to prevent Bank from
worsening Financial
Position

By: Basava Uppin

Consolidation of RRBs
Present Status: 43 RRBs have been merged into 28 RRBs in recent
times for their consolidation.
Proposal: Merge multiple RRBs into a single RRB at the state level.
Recent Decision: 26 RRBs across 11 states to be merged to form “One
State, One RRB”.
Example: 	Chaitanya 	Godavari 	Grameena 	Bank, 	Andhra 	Pragathi
Grameena 	Bank, 	Saptagiri 	Grameena 	Bank 	and 	Andhra 	Pradesh
Grameena Vikas Bank sponsored by Union Bank of India, Canara Bank,
Indian Bank and State Bank of India amalgamated into a single RRB
called as Andhra Pradesh Grameena Bank. 	Practice MCQ No. 57
Consider the following statements related to Regional Rural Banks:
1. 	The RRBs are not allowed to set up branches in Urban Areas.
2. 	The RRBs are required to give at least 40% of the loans in the form of priority
sectors.
3. 	The combined shareholding of the central government and the sponsor bank
cannot be less than 51%.

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements related to India Post Payment Bank (IPPB): 1. The India Post Payment Bank (IPPB) is a Public Limited Company under the Ministry of Finance. 2. The IPPB leverages the existing network of Post Offices to provide Banking services to the people. 3. The IPPB accepts both Savings and Fixed Deposit from the people. Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
context:
By: Basava Uppin	Criteria 	Payment Bank 	Small Finance Bank
Can give loans? 	No. But they can give loans to their employees.
In Prelims, always consider that they cannot give loans.
Yes. Minimum 50% of the loans should be below Rs
25 Lakhs.
Can Issue Debit Card? 	Yes.
Can Issue Credit Card? 	No as they cannot give loans. 	Yes.
Can Provide Cross border
Remittances?
Yes. 	Yes.
PSL Target 	No PSL Target as they cannot give loans.
Yes.	Yes.	CRR Applicable?
How do they make
profits?
Payment Banks accept Deposits but cannot give loans.
They make profits through:
1. 	SLR Requirement for Payment Banks is 75%. For all
other banks, SLR is 40%.
2. 	Entire SLR is maintained in the form of G-Secs.
Hence, Payment Banks invest 75% of Depositors’
money in G-Secs and hence make profits.
3. 	Remaining Depositor's money 	can be deposited
with other Banks to make profits.
Just like normal Banks, the SFBs accept deposits and
give loans. They make profits from the loans.
Yes.
Target: 60%. Reduced from 75% to 60% in Jun 2025.
Examples 	Airtel, Paytm, India Post Payment Bank, Jio and NSDL
Payment Bank Ujjivan SFB, Utkarsh SFB, Capital SFB, etc.
Branch Norms 	Unlike normal Banks, not required to open 25% branches
in Unbanked areas. 	However, need to have 25% access
points in the form of Business Correspondents, Customer
service centres in Rural Areas.
Must open 25% branches in Unbanked areas.

Practice MCQ No. 60
Consider the following statements related to India Post Payment Bank (IPPB):
1. 	The India Post Payment Bank (IPPB) is a Public Limited Company under the
Ministry of Finance.
2. 	The IPPB leverages the existing network of Post Offices to provide Banking
services to the people.
3. 	The IPPB accepts both Savings and Fixed Deposit from the people.
Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 only 	(b) 2 only 	(c) 3 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 3
Practice MCQ No. 62
Which among the following products/services can be offered by the Payment Banks in
India?
1. 	Act as Business Correspondents of other Banks
2. 	Acceptance of NRI Deposits
3. 	Marketing and Distribution of third-party Financial Products such as Insurance
and Mutual Funds.
4. 	Facilitate Cross Border Remittances
5. 	Acceptance of Fixed Deposits
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
(a) 1, 2 and 3 only 	(b) 1, 3 and 4 only 	(c) 3, 4 and 5 only (d) 1,2,3,4 and 5
Prelims MCQ No. 58
With reference to Payment banks in India, consider the following statements:
1. 	The Payment banks cannot accept deposits of more than Rs 1 lakh
from a single customer.
2. 	The Payment banks can issue both debit and credit cards.
3. 	The payment banks are required to invest minimum 75% of its
deposits in the Government Securities (G-Secs).
How many of the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) Only One 	(b) Only Two 	(c) All Three 	(d) None
Practice MCQ No. 59
Consider the following statements:
Statement I: Small Finance Banks are called as ‘Small” since they have certain
restrictions.
Statement II: Small Finance Banks cannot accept deposits of more than Rs 2 lakhs
Statement III: At least 50% of loans given by Small Finance Banks should be
below Rs 25 lakhs.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
a) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain
Statement I
b) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains
Statement I
c) Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
d) Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct
Practice MCQ No. 61
Consider the following statements related to Small Finance Banks (SFBs):
1. 	The Small Finance Banks (SFBs) can automatically transition to become Universal
Banks after having successful track record in last 5 years.
2. 	The Small Finance Banks are not allowed to accept time deposits.
3. 	The Small Finance Banks are required to give at least 75% of loans to priority
sectors.
Which among the statements given above is/are incorrect?
(a) 1 and 2 only 	(b) 1 and 3 only 	(c) 3 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 3

Examples 	Airtel, Paytm, India Post Payment Bank, Jio and NSDL
Payment Bank Ujjivan SFB, Utkarsh SFB, Capital SFB, etc.
Branch Norms 	Unlike normal Banks, not required to open 25% branches
in Unbanked areas. 	However, need to have 25% access
points in the form of Business Correspondents, Customer
service centres in Rural Areas.
Must open 25% branches in Unbanked areas.

Recent Developments:
Budget 2026 Announcement:
• 	All payments due from CPSEs to MSMEs must be processed through
TReDS platform
• 	Introduce CGTMSE-backed credit guarantee support for invoice discounting
on TReDS platform. This means that if the buyer fails to repay, CGTMSE will
cover the loss.
• 	Integrate GeM with TReDS to enable information-sharing with financiers.
• 	Introduce 	TReDS 	receivables 	as 	asset-backed 	securities 	to 	deepen 	the
secondary market.
2025: DTX becomes the 5th TReDS Platform in India. Other Platforms include
1. 	Receivables Exchange of India
2. 	Invoice mart
3. 	M1xchange (Mynd Online Exchange)
4. 	C2treds
2025: Earlier, it was mandatory for all CPSEs and companies with turnover of
more than Rs 500 crores to get registered on TReDS Platform as buyers. Now,
threshold for mandatory registration has got reduced from Rs 500 crores to Rs
250 crores.
2023: RBI allowed Insurance Companies to act as 4th Participants on the TReDS
Platform.
2021: Factoring Regulation Amendment Act, 2021: Earlier, only Banks and NBFC-
Factors were allowed to act as Financers. But the act allowed other NBFCs to act
as Financers as well.
Businessline, Jan 2025
Business Standard, March 2025

By: Basava UppinImportant Prelims Pointers on Micro Finance
1. 	99% of Micro-finance loans are provided to women
2. 	98% of the loans are provided through Joint Liability groups
(JLGs)
3. 	Loans are primarily taken for income generating activities but
are also taken for household expenses like Education, Health
4. 	Except Non-profit MFIs, all other MFIs are regulated by RBI.
RBI’s Guidelines on Micro Finance Sector (2022)
Applicability: Applicable to all Banks including NBFCs-MFIs
Definition of Micro-Finance Loan: Collateral free loan (irrespective
of end use) given to households having household income below Rs
3 lakh.
Limit on Loan: Loan repayment obligation should not be more than
50% of monthly income of borrower.
Interest rate on Loans: De-regulated (Market-Determined)
New Definition of NBFC-MFI: Min. 60% of the Assets should be in
the form of Micro-finance Loans. (Earlier, it was 75%)
BL, June 2026
PIB, 21st March 2026
Main Purpose: Extend Guarantee on the repayment of loans extended by the Banks to NBFC-MFIs.
Who will provide guarantee?: National Credit Guarantee Trustee Company Limited (NCGTC)
How much guarantee is provided?: Up to 80% of the loan amount
Who will pay the guarantee fee?: Banks
Benefit: Encourage Banks to give more loans to the NBFC-MFIs

Inland waterways.

National Bank for Financing Infrastructure
Development (NaBFID)
Established
under

Shared of

held by
Centre; Banks; Financial Institutions; Multilateral Institutions; Sovereign
Wealth Funds, Insurance and Pension funds etc.

financing.

or partly in India.
(a) 	Extend Loans

Funds
Raise money either in Rupees or foreign currencies.

By: Basava Uppin
Practice MCQ No. 67
With respect to Gold Loans, consider the following statements:
1. 	The Gold Loans can be given by both Banks and NBFCs, including Payment Banks.
2. 	The rate of Interest on Gold Loans is generally lower as compared to unsecured
loans.
3. 	The Gold Loans can be given against the collateral of Primary Gold as well as Gold
Jewellery.
4. 	The Gold Loans can be considered as priority sector loans, provided they fulfil the
criteria.
Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1, 2 and 3 only 	(b) 2, 3 and 4 only 	(c) 2 and 4 only 	(d) 1, 2, 3 and 4
Details about Gold Loans
Who can lend Gold Loans?: All regulated entities (Banks and NBFCs).
Underlying collateral: Gold/Silver Jewellery, Ornaments and Coins. Loans cannot be
given against primary gold, silver or financial assets backed by Gold such as Gold ETFs.
Can Loan be given against Gold coin? Yes. Provided Gold Coin is issued by Banks.
Loan-to-Value Ratio: Depends upon value of Loans.
• 	Up to Rs 2.5 lakhs: 85% of Gold’s value
• 	2.5 to 5 lakhs: 80% of Gold’s value
• 	Above Rs 5 lakhs: 75%
Is Proof of Gold ownership needed?: Yes.
Limit on Quantity of Gold Loans: Gold Ornaments (up to 1 kg) and Gold Coins (upto 50
grams)
Purpose: Both Consumption and Investment loans.
Tenor of Loans: Consumption loans are capped at 12 months.
Repayment flexibility: Lumpsum (Bullet Repayment) or Instalments.
Cash Disbursal Limit on Gold Loans: Rs 20,000. Remaining loan amount should be
transferred to bank account.
Can Gold loans be considered as Priority sector loans?: Yes. Provided the loans are
given to the priority sector. However, Loans against gold jewellery given by banks to
NBFCs are not eligible for priority sector status
April 2025
Jan 2025

By: Basava Uppin
Master 	Direction 	- 	Reserve 	Bank 	of 	India 	(Interest 	Rate 	on 	Deposits)
Directions, 2025
Background: Earlier, RBI used to prescribe the interest rates on the deposits.
However, the Interest rates for the Term deposits and Savings deposits have
been deregulated since 1997 and 2011 respectively.
Prohibition: Banks are prohibited from accepting interest free deposits except
Current Account.
Are the Interest rate deregulated for all types of accounts?: Yes. However, the
RBI prescribes the ceiling on the Interest rates for the FCNR Accounts.
No Discrimination: Uniform interest rates across all their branches for deposits
of the same amount and tenor, accepted on the same date. However, Banks
can offer higher interest rates to senior citizens, Bank staff etc.
Differential Rate of Interest may be offered by the Banks on Bulk deposits.
Minimum tenure of Fixed Deposit should be 7 days.
Hindu: April 2025
Practice MCQ No.71
Consider the following statements:
1. 	The Non-Resident (External) account is generally opened by NRIs to transfer
their income earned in other countries to India.
2. 	The Non-Resident (Ordinary) Account is generally opened by NRIs to transfer
their income earned in India to Bank account.
3. 	The Foreign Currency Non-Resident (FCNR) Bank account can be both Savings
account and Fixed Deposit account.
Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 only 	(b) 1 and 2 only 	(c) 3 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 3
Practice MCQ No. 73
Consider the following statements:
Statement-I: 	Rupee Depreciation may lead to surge in NRI Deposits with the
Indian Banks.
Statement-II: Rupee Depreciation automatically leads to increase in Interest rates
on the NRI Deposits.
A. Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct
explanation for Statement-I.
B. Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is not the
correct explanation for Statement-I.
C. Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
D. Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct.
Practice MCQ No.72
Consider the following statements:
1. 	Banks can accept interest free deposits only in current account.
2. 	Interest rate on all deposits, including FCNR Deposits have been deregulated.
3. 	Banks are allowed to offer Differential rate of Interest on Bulk Deposits.
Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only 	(b) 2 and 3 only 	(c) 1 and 3 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 3

• 	Reduce Burden on the Regulators

By: Basava Uppin	RBI’s Norms on Related Party Lending
Connected Lending
Meaning of Connected Lending 	Lending to persons who are able to control or influence the decision of the lender.
Examples 	Granting of loans to Directors of the bank or their relatives
Granting of loans to Firms owned by the Directors or in which directors have substantial interest.
Present Restrictions 	Presently, the banks are restricted from undertaking connected Lending as it leads to conflict of Interest.
Recent Controversy 	Chanda Kochhar ICICI Bank Loan Scam (2018): As CEO of ICICI Bank, Chanda Kochhar sanctioned loans to Videocon company
which then invested money in her husband’s company.
Recent Decision of RBI (2026) 	Existing 	guidelines 	are 	limited 	in 	scope 	and 	are 	not 	uniformly 	applicable 	to 	all 	regulated 	entities. 	Hence, 	need 	for
comprehensive framework

(c) 	Bank sanctioning loans to Board of directors or their family members
(d) 	Bank partnering with another Bank to give loans to its customers.

Practice MCQ No. 76
Which among the following best explains the “Expected Credit Loss” approach
to be adopted by the Banks for the Provisioning Coverage ratio?
(a) 	This approach requires the Banks to have same Provisioning coverage
ratio for all types of loans
(b) 	This approach prevents the Banks from keeping aside profits until the
loans turn into NPAs.
(c) 	This approach requires banks to estimate for potential future credit losses
and accordingly set aside capital.
(d) 	This approach does not require the Banks to set aside capital for the
Standard Loans.

(d) 	Neither 1 nor 2

Criteria 	Present Method 	Proposed Method
Approach 	Incurred loss Approach 	Expected Credit Loss Approach
Nature 	Reactive i.e., Banks set aside more capital
when Loans turn into NPAs.
Proactive i.e., Banks set aside more
capital even before loans turn into NPAs.
How is percentage of
Provisioning amount
decided?
Based upon the stage of Loans- Standard,
Sub-Standard, Doubtful and Lost
Loans would be categorized into three
stages depending upon their risk profile.
Problem/ Advantage 	Problem:
Banks set aside more capital when loan
turns into NPA after 90 days. Hence,
sudden increase in Provisioning Capital
requirement may make it difficult for the
Bank to meet provisioning norms.
Advantage:
Banks set aside more capital based upon
expected credit loss. Hence, Banks would
have already kept aside more capital by
the time loan turns into NPAs.
Note: Do not unnecessarily waste time on understanding Expected Credit Loss approach as it is still in the proposal stage. UPSC may ask only a single-
liner question on the proposed approach.
Business Standard: Oct 2025

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements related to Risk weightage norms laid down by the RBI: 1. The Risk weightage percentage cannot be more than 100%. 2. Increase in Risk weightage percentage may lead to increase in rate of Interest on loans. 3. The Risk weightage for Microfinance loans is higher as compared to unsecured personal loans. Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
context:
By: Basava Uppin
RBI’s rules on Liquidity Coverage Ratio (2025)
Background: Collapse of Banks such as Silicon Valley Bank due to sudden withdrawal of Deposits leading to Bank run.
Meaning of Run-off: Estimation of how much Deposits would leave the Bank during crisis either through withdrawal or transfer
Impact of Digital Banking on Run off: Easier for the people to transfer the deposits from Bank “X” to Bank ”Y” in case of crisis in Bank “X”. Hence, Digital Banking
has increased Run-off factor.
RBI’s Guidelines: Higher run-off for the deposits accepted through Internet and Mobile Banking Facilities. (Details are not important for Prelims)

question: Which among the following constitute priority sector loans under Agriculture? 1. Loans under Kisan Credit Card 2. Loans against Negotiable Warehousing receipts (NWRs) 3. Loans to agricultural start-ups and Food processing Industries. Select the correct answer using code given below:
context:
By: Basava Uppin
Co-Lending On-Lending
Advantages:
Bank: Capitalize on reach of NBFCs + Fulfill PSL Target
NBFCs: Provide more loans in partnership with Banks
Customers: Lower rate of Interest as compared to loans from
NBFCs alone.
RBI’s New Guidelines
Scope: Earlier, co-lending was between Banks and NBFCs. Now, it
can be between any Regulated Entities.
Minimum percentage of loan by one single entity reduced from
20% to 10%.
Rate of Interest: Blended rate of Interest
Priority sector Lending:
Agriculture, MSMEs, Education, Housing, Social Infra,
Renewable Energy, Loans to poor and Vulnerable sections
etc.
Advantages:
Bank: Capitalize on reach of NBFCs + Fulfill PSL Target
NBFCs: Get Capital from Bank
Students’ Doubts
Doubt 1: Do NBFCs have PSL Target?
Basava’s response: No.
Doubt 2: Is On-lending applicable only for PSL
Loans?
Basava’s response: Not Necessarily. Banks can
lend money to NBFCs for other purposes as well.
Doubt 3: Are the entire loans given by Banks and
NBFCs 	together 	counted 	as 	PSL 	loans 	for 	the
Banks?
Basava’s response: No. The Banks would count
only their share of loans as PSL Loans

subject: Economy
question: The Priority sector lending requirements are applicable to how many among the following categories of institutions? 1. Urban Cooperative Banks 2. Foreign Banks 3. Local Area Banks 4. Small Finance Banks 5. Payment Banks 6. NBFCs Select the correct answer using the code given below:
context:
By: Basava Uppin
Co-Lending On-Lending
Advantages:
Bank: Capitalize on reach of NBFCs + Fulfill PSL Target
NBFCs: Provide more loans in partnership with Banks
Customers: Lower rate of Interest as compared to loans from
NBFCs alone.
RBI’s New Guidelines
Scope: Earlier, co-lending was between Banks and NBFCs. Now, it
can be between any Regulated Entities.
Minimum percentage of loan by one single entity reduced from
20% to 10%.
Rate of Interest: Blended rate of Interest
Priority sector Lending:
Agriculture, MSMEs, Education, Housing, Social Infra,
Renewable Energy, Loans to poor and Vulnerable sections
etc.
Advantages:
Bank: Capitalize on reach of NBFCs + Fulfill PSL Target
NBFCs: Get Capital from Bank
Students’ Doubts
Doubt 1: Do NBFCs have PSL Target?
Basava’s response: No.
Doubt 2: Is On-lending applicable only for PSL
Loans?
Basava’s response: Not Necessarily. Banks can
lend money to NBFCs for other purposes as well.
Doubt 3: Are the entire loans given by Banks and
NBFCs 	together 	counted 	as 	PSL 	loans 	for 	the
Banks?
Basava’s response: No. The Banks would count
only their share of loans as PSL Loans

By: Basava Uppin
Salient Features of Integrated Ombudsman Scheme (2026)
Meaning of
Ombudsman
Government official/agency before whom Complaints can be filed.
Role of Integrated
Ombudsman
Integrated Ombudsman is senior official appointed by the RBI before whom
complaints can be filed. RBI may appoint one or more of its officers as RBI
Ombudsman and RBI Deputy Ombudsman
On What grounds
complaints can be
filed?
Complaints can be filed for deficiency in services such as delay, non-adherence
to working hours, refusal to open Bank account without any valid reason etc.
Complaints can be
filed against?
Banks, NBFCs, Credit Information Companies and Pre-Paid Payment Issuers.
Note: Even Non-Scheduled Urban Cooperative Banks are also included.
When can
complaint be filed?
One must first file a complaint before the concerned Bank. However, if the
Bank does not take any action within 1 month or if you are not satisfied with
the 	reply 	given 	by 	the 	Bank, 	then 	you 	can 	file 	complaint 	before 	RBI’s
Ombudsman.
Note: There is no limit on the amount in a dispute that can be brought before
the RBI
Mode for
registering
complaints
Centralised Receipt and Processing Centre to be set up by RBI. Complaints can
be filed in both online (E-mail) and Physical mode.
Limits on
compensation
payable to
Customer
Rs 30 Lakhs. (Earlier, Rs 20 Lakhs)
In addition, the RBI Ombudsman shall also have the power to provide a
compensation up to ₹3 lakh for the loss of the Complainant’s time, expenses
incurred, harassment/mental anguish suffered, etc.
The Hindu, Jan 2026
Prelims 2010
With reference to the institution of Banking Ombudsman in
India, which one of the statements is not correct?
(a) 	The Banking Ombudsman is appointed by the RBI
(b) 	The Banking ombudsman can receive complaints from
individuals non-resident in India.
(c) 	The orders passed by Ombudsman are final and binding
on the parties concerned.
(d) 	The service provided by the Banking Ombudsman is free
of any fee.
Practice MCQ No. 92
With reference to the RBI’s Integrated Ombudsman Scheme,
which one of the statements is not correct?
(a) 	Under this scheme, customers can file complaint against
Banks, NBFCs, Credit Information Companies and Pre-
paid Payment issuers.
(b) 	Complaints can be filed only against Scheduled Banks.
(c) 	There is no limit on the amount in a dispute that can be
brought before the RBI
(d) 	The maximum compensation which can be awarded by
the Ombudsman is Rs 30 lakhs.

One must first file a complaint before the concerned Bank. However, if the
Bank does not take any action within 1 month or if you are not satisfied with
the 	reply 	given 	by 	the 	Bank, 	then 	you 	can 	file 	complaint 	before 	RBI’s
Ombudsman.
Note: There is no limit on the amount in a dispute that can be brought before
the RBI
Mode for
registering
complaints
Centralised Receipt and Processing Centre to be set up by RBI. Complaints can
be filed in both online (E-mail) and Physical mode.
Limits on
compensation
payable to
Customer
Rs 30 Lakhs. (Earlier, Rs 20 Lakhs)
In addition, the RBI Ombudsman shall also have the power to provide a
compensation up to ₹3 lakh for the loss of the Complainant’s time, expenses
incurred, harassment/mental anguish suffered, etc.

Background

By: Basava Uppin	Merchant Discount Rate (MDR)
Accepts Payment through
Debit Card/Credit Card/UPI
• 	Merchant 	Accepting 	Payment
pays 	Merchant 	Discount 	Rate
(MDR) to his Bank i.e., HDFC
• 	MDR is fixed by the RBI.
• 	Ranges between 0.40% to 0.95%
of 	value 	of 	the 	Transaction
depending upon (a) Category of
Merchant 	(b) 	Mode 	of
Acceptance 	i.e., 	Physical 	PoS 	or
QR Code
Customer’s
Bank: ICICI
Merchant’s Bank: HDFC
Payment System
Operator such as
MasterCard, Visa,
RuPay
• 	MDR 	is 	shared 	between 	Merchant’s 	Bank, 	Customer’s 	Bank 	and 	Payment 	System
Operator.
• 	The 	proportion 	of 	MDR 	which 	gets 	shared 	with 	the 	Customers’ 	Bank 	is 	called 	as
Interchange Fee.
• 	It is fixed by the Payment System Operator (PSO) i.e. Card Networks such as Mastercard
and Visa or NPCI (UPI, Rupay Card)
• 	Card Issuers compete with each other to offer more Interchange fee to the Bank and
hence acts as main incentive for the Banks.
• 	For example, if Mastercard offers more interchange fee to ICICI Bank as compared to
Visa, then ICICI Bank would offer Mastercard Debit Card/Credit Card instead of Visa
Debit/Credit Card.

Bank: HDFC
Customers’
Bank: HDFC

Fee

machines and debit cards.
Industry has been demanding for MDR on UPI
Transactions to reduce concentration risk and promote
smaller Apps.

• 	Cap on UPI Market Share
• 	Concept of MDR and Interchange Fee on UPI Transactions
Background

By: Basava Uppin	Merchant Discount Rate (MDR)
Accepts Payment through

• 	Merchant 	Accepting 	Payment
pays 	Merchant 	Discount 	Rate
(MDR) to his Bank i.e., HDFC

of 	value 	of 	the 	Transaction
depending upon (a) Category of

• 	MDR 	is 	shared 	between 	Merchant’s 	Bank, 	Customer’s 	Bank 	and 	Payment 	System
Operator.
• 	The 	proportion 	of 	MDR 	which 	gets 	shared 	with 	the 	Customers’ 	Bank 	is 	called 	as
Interchange Fee.
• 	It is fixed by the Payment System Operator (PSO) i.e. Card Networks such as Mastercard
and Visa or NPCI (UPI, Rupay Card)
• 	Card Issuers compete with each other to offer more Interchange fee to the Bank and
hence acts as main incentive for the Banks.
• 	For example, if Mastercard offers more interchange fee to ICICI Bank as compared to
Visa, then ICICI Bank would offer Mastercard Debit Card/Credit Card instead of Visa
Debit/Credit Card.

subject: Economy
question: In the context of inflation, the momentum effect reflects/captures the:

options.a: the impact of price levels from the previous year’s base period on the current year-on-year inflation calculation.
options.b: the inflationary pressure caused by an increase in the speed at which money circulates in the economy.
options.c: the sudden spikes in food and fuel prices driven specifically by supply-side shocks.
options.d: month-on-month price changes in the current year. • Momentum effect captures the month-on-month price changes in the current year • Base effect reflects the influence of price movements in the corresponding months of the previous year on year-on-year inflation.

context:
By: Basava Uppin	Domestic Inflation
Trends in WPI and CPI
• 	Steady decline in the CPI in last 4 years. April-December 2025
marked the lowest average inflation rate in the current CPI series.
• 	Wholesale Price Index (WPI)-based inflation has consistently been
lower than CPI inflation in these years
• 	WPI rate of Inflation turned negative in 2023-24.
Trends in Headline and Core Inflation
• 	Decline in Headline Inflation due to decline in Food Inflation.
• 	Food Inflation has remained deflationary since June 2025
• 	Key drivers for fall in Food Inflation: Pulses, Vegetables and Spices.
• 	Core 	Inflation 	has 	remained 	relatively 	stable 	and 	has 	moderately
increased in April-December 2025
• 	Increase in Core Inflation mainly due to increase in prices of Gold and
Silver

By: Basava Uppin	Domestic Inflation

the:
(a) 	the impact of price levels from the previous year’s base period

(b) 	the inflationary pressure caused by an increase in the speed at
which money circulates in the economy.
(c) 	the sudden spikes in food and fuel prices driven specifically by
supply-side shocks.
(d) 	month-on-month price changes in the current year.

• 	Base effect reflects the influence of price movements in the corresponding months
of the previous year on year-on-year inflation.

kept the current year inflation lower despite decrease in prices.
