question: With respect to Initial Public Offer (IPO), consider the following statements: 1. Only Shares can be issued by companies in IPO. 2. Certain percentage of shares have to be allotted to retail investors in IPO. 3. There is a lock-in period of 30 days for the retail investors. Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
context:
By: Basava Uppin
Business line, June 2025
Prelims Pointers on Employee Stock Option Plan
Meaning: Companies offer employees the right to buy
shares at a discounted/nil price after a specific period.
Eligibility:
• 	Permanent Employees
• 	Directors of Company
• 	Contractual Employees in certain conditions.
• 	Promoters of Start ups
Vesting Period: Minimum 1 year.
Exercise price: Company has flexibility to decide on
price.
Earlier Situation
Founders of Start-ups
Gets ESOP to compensate for lower salary
Founder is categorized as Promoter
Definition of Promoter of Company
A promoter is a person who:
(a) Has been named as promoter in offer documents
(b) Has control over the affairs of the company
(c) Is in accordance with whose advice, directions, or
instructions the Board of Directors acts
Note: Person is categorised as Promoter not just based
upon 	shareholding 	but 	also 	based 	upon 	ability 	to
influence decisions of company.
Not allowed to retain ESOP when company goes for IPO
If Founder is not categorized as Promoter
Allowed to retain ESOP
Problems
Ø 	Founders decided not to classify themselves as Promoters (Ex: Paytm, Zomato, Swiggy
Ø 	Flipping of Start-ups due to poor ease of Doing business
SEBI’s New Guidelines
Promoters of Start-ups are allowed to retain ESOPs provided the ESOPs are granted 1 year
before IPO.
Money control, Sep 2025

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements: 1. Retail investors through demat account can invest in Treasury Bills and Government of India Debt Bonds in primary market. 2. The Negotiated Dealing System-Order Matching is a government securities trading platform of the Reserve Bank of India. 3. The Central Depository Services Ltd. Is jointly promoted by the Reserve Bank of India and the Bombay Stock Exchange. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
context:
By: Basava Uppin
Prelims Pointers on Common Contract Note
Meaning of Contract note: Document issued by brokers to investors,
summarising all trades executed on a trading day. It includes details such
as the security name, quantity, trade price, time, brokerage charges etc.
Present status: Currently, if an investor splits trades between the two
exchanges, NSE and BSE, they receive separate contract notes from each.
Issues with separate contract notes: Leads to operational burden on the
investors.
Salient features of Common Contract Note:
• 	One common contract note across all stock exchanges and all trades.
• 	Introduced with Single volume weighted average prices across all
stock exchanges.
• 	Made available to all the categories of investors.
• 	Common Contract Note is broker-specific. Hence, If the transaction is
carried out through multiple brokers, different brokers will provide
different common contract note.
Prelims Pointers on NIFTY Waves Index
Details: Track the performance of stocks that represent the Indian media,
entertainment and gaming industry.
Launched by: NSE
Base Year: 2005 with value of 1000.
Eligible entities: All companies domiciled in India and traded (listed & traded
and not listed but permitted to trade) at NSE at the time of review are eligible
for inclusion in the index.
Weightage of shares: Based upon Market Capitalisation subject to a cap of 5%.

aggregate the demand and supply and

Organization which holds securities (like shares,
debentures, 	bonds, 	government 	securities,
mutual fund units etc.) of investors in electronic
form.

Promoted by NSE. Yet to listed on the stock
exchange.

Promoted by BSE. Largest depository in terms
of 	demat 	accounts. 	Already 	listed 	on 	stock
exchange. Prelims 2021

securities sold by him.
Example: 	Indian 	Clearing 	Corporation, 	NSE

By: Basava Uppin	Terms related to Stock Market in news
Market Capitalization Meaning: Calculated as ( Number of shares multiplier by their prices). Fall in market capitalization to below $ 5.3 trillion in Mar 2026.
Top 5 Countries in terms of Market Capitalization: US, China, Japan, Hong Kong, and India
Alpha and Beta
Alpha: Excess returns earned by an investment compared to its benchmark index. If the alpha number is Positive, then Share has outperformed
Benchmark index.
Beta (Prelims 2023): Measures the volatility in the share price as compared to Benchmark. The market index has a beta of 1. So, an asset with a
beta of more than 1 is more volatile than that index, while an asset with a beta of less than 1 is less volatile
T + 0 Settlement
Background: Presently, India follows T+1 settlement cycle.
Latest Development: From March 2024, the T+0 Settlement will be implemented in a phased manner. In the later phase, it would be
introducing Instant Settlement.
Countries offering T+0 Settlement: Russia Taiwan and South Korea.
Pros of T+0 Settlement: Increased liquidity in market + Increase in Trading volume
Cons of T+0 settlement: Higher market Volatility
Profit Booking Sale of the shares to realize the appreciation in their 	value. For example, Let's say a person has bought a share of a company X at Rs 10 and
later sells it at Rs 15 to book profits. Profit booking may lead to volatility in stock market.
VIX Index (India Volatility
Index)
• 	Published by NSE. Measures the degree of volatility or fluctuations in the Nifty over the next 30 days.
• 	Higher value of VIX Index indicates higher volatility expectations, while a lower value of India VIX indicates lower volatility expectations.
• 	VIX Index has a strong negative correlation with Nifty. Every time VIX Index falls

question: With respect to Specialized Investment Funds (SIFs) introduced by SEBI, consider the following statements: 1. The Specialized Investment Funds (SIFs) are the Mutual funds with smaller SIPs to to promote “Sachetisation” of Mutual Funds. 2. Unlike regular mutual funds, investors get tax incentives on SIFs. 3. Unlike regular mutual funds, SIFs are more flexible and riskier. Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
context:
By: Basava Uppin
• 	Pooled Investment vehicle
• 	Investment in standard assets such as
Shares and Bonds.
• 	Investors do not directly own the assets,
rather they own the units of Mutual Fund.
Pros
• 	Minimum Investment of Rs 500 and
hence suited to retail investors
• 	Low Risk: Standard Investment strategy of
investing in shares and Bonds
Mutual
Funds
Cons
• 	Lack 	of 	Customization 	as 	the
investment is same for all investors.
• 	Lack of Flexibility to Fund managers
in designing schemes
• 	Low returns
Portfolio
Management
Service (PMS)
• 	Investor’s Portfolio is managed by Portfolio
Manager
• 	Investment in standard assets such as
Shares and Bonds and high-risk
instruments such as Derivatives
• 	Investors directly own the assets
Pros
• 	Portfolio 	is 	customized 	as 	per 	the
requirements of the investor.
• 	Higher flexibility to the portfolio manager
in managing portfolio
• 	High Returns.
Cons
• 	Minimum Investment of Rs 50 Lakhs
• 	Higher 	risk 	due 	to 	investment 	in
riskier assets.
• 	Unsuitable 	to 	investors 	willing 	to
take moderate risk.
Specialized
Investment
Fund (SIF)
• 	Pooled Investment Vehicle like a Mutual
Fund
• 	Investment 	in 	standard 	assets 	such 	as
Shares 	and 	Bonds 	and 	high-risk
instruments such as Derivatives
• 	Investors do not directly own the assets,
rather they own the units of SIF.
• 	Minimum Investment of Rs 10 lakhs.
Pros
• 	Hybrid of Mutual Fund and PMS.
• 	Features 	of 	Mutual 	Fund: 	Pooled
Investment 	vehicle, 	Standardization 	of
investment strategies as per SEBI
• 	Features of PMS: More Flexibility to SIF in
designing 	schemes 	as 	compared 	to
Mutual 	Funds. 	For 	example, 	they 	can
launch Long-short Equity Funds.
Cons
• 	Lack 	of 	Customization 	as 	the
investment is same for all investors.
Specialized Investment Fund
Business line, Jan 9, 2025

By: Basava UppinSpecialized Investment Fund
Prelims Pointers on Specialized Investment Fund
Minimum investment: 	Rs 10 lakhs. 	However, this limit is not applicable
to accredited investors.
How are they similar to Mutual Funds?: Both Mutual Funds and SIFs are
pooled investment vehicles.
How are they different from Mutual Funds?
• 	Minimum 	investment 	in 	Mutual 	Funds 	is 	Rs 	500 	while 	minimum
investment in SIF is Rs 10 lakhs.
• 	Unlike regular Mutual Funds, the SIF are allowed to launch Long-short
Equity Funds (similar to Hedge Funds)
• 	Unlike regular Mutual Funds, the SIF provide more more flexibility.
Eligibility criteria for launching SIF: Only mutual funds operational for at
least 	three 	years 	with 	₹10,000 	crore 	average 	AUM 	(Assets 	Under
Management) in the preceding three years can launch SIF.
Taxation Regime: Same as Mutual Funds.
Practice MCQ No.98
With respect to Specialized Investment Funds (SIFs) introduced by
SEBI, consider the following statements:
1. 	The Specialized Investment Funds (SIFs) are the Mutual funds
with smaller SIPs to to promote “Sachetisation” of Mutual Funds.
2. 	Unlike regular mutual funds, investors get tax incentives on SIFs.
3. 	Unlike regular mutual funds, SIFs are more flexible and riskier.
Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only 	(b) 2 and 3 only 	(c) 3 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 3

Prelims MCQ No. 100
Which among the following best describes the concept of a Virtual Power Purchase
Agreement (VPPA), recently seen in news?
(a) 	A contract involving physical delivery of renewable electricity from generator to
buyer at a pre-fixed tariff.
(b) 	A derivative contract regulated by SEBI where electricity prices are settled purely
in cash without linkage to physical generation.
(c) 	renewable 	energy 	supply 	agreement 	where 	the 	buyer 	receives 	electricity
physically through the grid but settles price differences virtually.
(d) 	A financial agreement where renewable power is sold in the market, and the
buyer settles price differences while receiving environmental attributes.

subject: Economy
question: With respect to Securitised Debt Instruments (SDIs), consider the following statements: 1. The Securitised Debt Instruments are the financial instruments which are issued against standard and Non-performing assets of the financial institutions. 2. Retail investors are prohibited from investing in SDIs due to their inherent risk. 3. The SDIs enable financial institutions to manage their liquidity and diversify their risk Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
context:
By: Basava Uppin
Background: Presently, the Option contracts in India have
weekly/monthly expiry. There is no one day Options Contracts.
Meaning: 	The 	0DTE 	options 	contracts 	are 	structured 	with
weekly expiries on each day of the week, Monday through
Friday.
Introduced by: NSE International Exchange (NSE-IX) based in
GIFT City
Working Mechanism: On each trading day, the applicable 0DTE
options contracts shall expire at 3.30 pm.
Note: SEBI is also planning to introduce long term options
contracts with maturities ranging from 1 to 3 years known as
LEAPS (Long Term Equity Anticipation Securities)
BL, July 2025
Temperature linked
Derivative
Water Bottling Company expects
Temperature to remain lower in May
leading to lower sales and profits
Bank/Financial Institution expects
temperature to increase in May
leading to higher sales and Profits
for Water Bottling Company
Bisleri enters into contract with Bank
If Temperature remains lower 	If Temperature increases
Bank gives money to Bisleri 	Bisleri gives money to Bank
Bisleri Gains
Bank Loses
Bisleri loses
Bank Gains
Important Prelims Pointers on India’s first weather Derivative
Meaning: Weather derivatives are financial contracts whose
value 	depends 	on 	specific 	weather 	variables 	such 	as
Temperature, rainfall etc.
Difference 	between 	Weather 	Derivatives 	and 	Insurance:
Insurance 	provides 	protection 	against 	actual 	losses 	while
Derivatives provide protection against bad weather outcomes.
Hence, no proof of loss is needed in weather derivative.
Developed by: NCDEX in partnership with IMD.
Salient features:
• 	Included in Securities Contract Regulation act in 2024
• 	Location-specific and season-based contracts
• 	Traded on the stock exchanges and commodity exchanges
• 	Linked to meteorological parameters such as Temperature,
Rainfall etc. provided by IMD
• 	Eligible 	users 	can 	be 	farmers, 	Industries, 	Insurance
companies etc.

By: Basava UppinSecuritized Debt Instrument
Banks, NBFCs, HFCs, AIFIs
Standard Assets : Home Loan, Car
Loan, Personal Loan, Credit Card,
Infrastructure Loans, MSME Loans
Sell pool of standard Assets to a
Special purpose Entity (SPE)
Special Purpose Entity Issues
Securitized Debt Instruments (SDI)
against pool of standard Assets
Bought by Individuals, Banks,
NBFCs, Insurance & Pension fund
Companies, Mutual Fund
Companies
Prelims Pointers on Securitized Debt Instruments (SDIs)
Meaning: SDIs are the debt instruments which are issued against standard assets of the Banks, NBFCs,
HFCs and AIFIs.
Benefits for the Issuer: 	Convert illiquid assets into liquid assets + Diversification of risk as the default
on the standard loans get passed on to the investors.
Benefits for the Investor: Diversification of investment portfolio + Regular cash flow backed by EMIs
Regulatory Framework: Both RBI and SEBI
Can SDIs be issued against NPAs? No. Security receipts can be issued against NPAs.
Can Individuals buy SDIs?: Yes. 	However, RBI has mandated that minimum investment in SDIs should
be Rs 1 crore. Hence, participation of retail investors in SDIs is limited.
Practice MCQ No.101
With respect to Securitised Debt Instruments (SDIs), consider the following statements:
1. 	The Securitised Debt Instruments are the financial instruments which are issued against standard and
Non-performing assets of the financial institutions.
2. 	Retail investors are prohibited from investing in SDIs due to their inherent risk.
3. 	The SDIs enable financial institutions to manage their liquidity and diversify their risk
Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only 	(b) 2 and 3 only (c) 3 only (d) 1, 2 and 3
Business Standard, Nov 2024

By: Basava UppinMunicipal Bonds (Muni Bonds)
Indian Express, April 2025
What are Municipal Bonds?: Bonds issued by Urban Local Bodies (ULB) to raise
money infrastructure projects such as Roads, Bridges, Schools, Hospitals etc.
Types of Muni Bonds:
General obligation bonds: Money raised through these Bonds can be used for
creation and upgradation of any infrastructure. The money raised through
such Bonds is repaid from the overall revenues earned by the ULBs.
Revenue Bonds: Money raised through these Bonds can be used for a specific
infrastructure 	project 	such 	as 	Construction 	of 	metro. 	The 	money 	raised
through such Bonds is repaid only through the revenue earned from specific
infrastructure project. Ex: 	Bangalore Bus Fleet Expansion Bonds, Ghaziabad-
India’s first Green Municipal Revenue Bond etc.
Municipal Bond Market in India
• 	Bengaluru Municipal Corporation issued municipal bonds for the first time
in 1997 for financing city roads and drains. Subsequently, number of ULBs
have issued Muni Bonds.
• 	In 2015, SEBI came out with revised the guidelines for the issuance of
Municipal Bonds.
Recent Development (Oct 2025): Municipal Bonds can be used as collateral for
Repo and Reverse Repo Transactions.
Practice MCQ No.102
Consider the following statements with respect to Municipal Bonds
in India:

subject: Economy
question: With respect to Social Stock Exchanges, consider the following statements: 1. The Social Stock exchanges are set up as separate segment under the existing stock exchanges in India. 2. It is mandatory for both Not-for-Profit and For-Profit enterprises to register on the Social stock exchanges to raise capital. Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
context:
By: Basava Uppin
SEBI’s Framework on Social Stock Exchanges
Meaning: Social Stock Exchange enables entities such as
NGOs to raise finances for social sector projects such as
Education, Housing, Drinking water etc.
For example, Brazil's Socio-environmental Impact Exchange
(BVSA) was the first SSE. Some of the other SSEs across the
world include- UK Social Stock Exchange, South Africa's
SASIX, Canada's Social Venture Connexion.
Background: Social Stock exchange has been set up in India
based upon recommendations of Ishat Hussain Committee
Establishment: SSE has been set up as separate segment
under the existing stock exchanges such as BSE, NSE etc.
Hence, SSE would not be standalone exchanges, rather,
they would be set up under the existing stock exchanges
only.
BL: 2023 ET: Mar 19, 2025
Development Impact Bonds: Involving Private Sector,
Outcome Funder (Donor NGO/Charitable Foundation)
and Implementation Agency.
Social Venture Funds (SVFs): For profit Enterprises can raise
capital from SVFs. The SVFs are a category of AIFs that
invest in social enterprises (similar to VCF which invest in
start-ups.

By: Basava Uppin
Is it mandatory for a Not-for-profit organization to register in order to raise funds through Social Stock Exchange?: Yes
Is it mandatory to register for profit social enterprises in order to raise funds through Social Stock Exchange? No
What are the guidelines for issue of Zero Coupon Zero Principal Bonds?
• 	Zero Coupon Zero Principal Instruments must have specific tenure and can only be issued for a specific project or activity to be completed within a duration
• 	Minimum issue size for issue of Zero Coupon Zero Principal Instruments shall be rupees Rs 50 lakh and minimum application size shall be Rs 1000 (Earlier, it was Rs
10,000)
• 	It should be issued in Dematerialised Form only.
• 	Trading is not allowed
• 	Social Enterprise shall terminate the listing of Zero Coupon Zero Principal Instruments when it achieves its objective
• 	The introduction of SSEs in India has not witnessed any changes in the Income Tax Act as of now. However, considering that the activities NPOs are engaged in, tax
exemptions may be availed by the investors. 	For example, there would be no Capital Gains Tax and Securities Transaction Tax (STT). Similarly, investments in Zero
Coupon Zero Principal Bonds can be considered as donations.
FAQs on Social Stock Exchanges
Practice MCQ No.105
With respect to Social Stock Exchanges, consider the following statements:
1. 	The Social Stock exchanges are set up as separate segment under the
existing stock exchanges in India.
2. 	It is mandatory for both Not-for-Profit and For-Profit enterprises to
register on the Social stock exchanges to raise capital.
Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 only 	(b) 2 only 	(c) Both 1 and 2 	(d) Neither 1 nor 2
Practice MCQ No.106
With respect to Zero Coupon Zero Principal Bonds, consider the following statements:
1. 	These Bonds can be issued by both Not-for-Profit and For-Profit enterprises on the
Social Stock exchanges.
2. 	These Bonds must have specific tenure and can only be issued for a specific project
or activity
3. 	Individuals are not allowed to invest in these bonds.
How many statements given above is/are correct?
(a) Only One 	(b) Only Two 	(c) All Three 	(d) None
Practice MCQ No.108
Which among the following statements is incorrect with respect to Zero Coupon Zero
Principal (ZCZP) bonds?
(a) The ZCZP are form of traditional debt instruments used for raising finances for social
welfare.
(b) The ZCZP Bonds can be issued only by Not-for-Profit enterprises.
(c) The ZCZP Bonds must be issued only in Dematerialized form.
(d) The ZCZP Bonds cannot be traded.
Practice MCQ No.107
Which among the following financial instruments is/are part of the Social Stock
Exchanges in India?
1. 	Shares and Bonds
2. 	Social Venture Funds
3. 	Zero Coupon Zero Principal Bonds
4. 	Development Impact Bonds
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
(a) 1 only (b) 2, 3 and 4 only (c) 3 and 4 only 	(d) 1, 2, 3 and 4

By: Basava Uppin

subject: Economy
question: Which among the following financial instruments is/are part of the Social Stock Exchanges in India? 1. Shares and Bonds 2. Social Venture Funds 3. Zero Coupon Zero Principal Bonds 4. Development Impact Bonds Select the correct answer using the code given below:
context:
By: Basava Uppin
SEBI’s Framework on Social Stock Exchanges
Meaning: Social Stock Exchange enables entities such as
NGOs to raise finances for social sector projects such as
Education, Housing, Drinking water etc.
For example, Brazil's Socio-environmental Impact Exchange
(BVSA) was the first SSE. Some of the other SSEs across the
world include- UK Social Stock Exchange, South Africa's
SASIX, Canada's Social Venture Connexion.
Background: Social Stock exchange has been set up in India
based upon recommendations of Ishat Hussain Committee
Establishment: SSE has been set up as separate segment
under the existing stock exchanges such as BSE, NSE etc.
Hence, SSE would not be standalone exchanges, rather,
they would be set up under the existing stock exchanges
only.
BL: 2023 ET: Mar 19, 2025
Development Impact Bonds: Involving Private Sector,
Outcome Funder (Donor NGO/Charitable Foundation)
and Implementation Agency.
Social Venture Funds (SVFs): For profit Enterprises can raise
capital from SVFs. The SVFs are a category of AIFs that
invest in social enterprises (similar to VCF which invest in
start-ups.

By: Basava Uppin
Is it mandatory for a Not-for-profit organization to register in order to raise funds through Social Stock Exchange?: Yes
Is it mandatory to register for profit social enterprises in order to raise funds through Social Stock Exchange? No
What are the guidelines for issue of Zero Coupon Zero Principal Bonds?
• 	Zero Coupon Zero Principal Instruments must have specific tenure and can only be issued for a specific project or activity to be completed within a duration
• 	Minimum issue size for issue of Zero Coupon Zero Principal Instruments shall be rupees Rs 50 lakh and minimum application size shall be Rs 1000 (Earlier, it was Rs
10,000)
• 	It should be issued in Dematerialised Form only.
• 	Trading is not allowed
• 	Social Enterprise shall terminate the listing of Zero Coupon Zero Principal Instruments when it achieves its objective
• 	The introduction of SSEs in India has not witnessed any changes in the Income Tax Act as of now. However, considering that the activities NPOs are engaged in, tax
exemptions may be availed by the investors. 	For example, there would be no Capital Gains Tax and Securities Transaction Tax (STT). Similarly, investments in Zero
Coupon Zero Principal Bonds can be considered as donations.

subject: Economy
question: Which of the following is issued by registered foreign portfolio investors to overseas investors who want to be part of the Indian stock market without registering themselves directly?

options.d: Participatory Note SEBI’s Guidelines on Offshore Derivative Instruments (ODIs) 2 categories of FPI: • Category-I: Sovereign Wealth Funds, Government agencies, Central banks, Insurance, Pension Fund, Mutual Fund companies from FATF member countries. • Category-II: FPIs not registered under Category-I FPI, including individuals. Who can issue ODIs?: Only Category-I FPIs registered with SEBI. Do ODI Investor hold securities directly? No. FPI remains the owner of underlying security, but economic benefits are transferred to investor. Controversy: P-Notes can be used for money laundering through Round Tripping. Criteria for issuing ODIs: • FPIs must fulfill KYC norms to reduce anonymity of investors. • Monthly reporting of issuance of P-Notes Can P-Notes be traded outside India? Yes. But only the among the entities based in FATF member countries. SEBI’s Guidelines (2024) • FPIs issuing P-Notes shall have special type of registration with term “ODI” as suffix. • FPI cannot issue P-Notes against derivatives.

By: Basava Uppin

By: Basava Uppin
Prelims 2019
Which of the following is issued by registered foreign portfolio
investors to overseas investors who want to be part of the Indian
stock market without registering themselves directly?
(a) 	Certificate of Deposit
(b) 	Commercial Paper
(c) 	Promissory Note
(d) 	Participatory Note
SEBI’s Guidelines on Offshore Derivative Instruments (ODIs)
2 categories of FPI:
• 	Category-I: Sovereign Wealth Funds, Government agencies, Central
banks, Insurance, Pension Fund, Mutual Fund companies from FATF
member countries.
• 	Category-II: 	FPIs 	not 	registered 	under 	Category-I 	FPI, 	including
individuals.
Who can issue ODIs?: Only Category-I FPIs registered with SEBI.
Do ODI Investor hold securities directly? No. FPI remains the owner of
underlying security, but economic benefits are transferred to investor.
Controversy: P-Notes can be used for money laundering through Round
Tripping.
Criteria for issuing ODIs:
• 	FPIs must fulfill KYC norms to reduce anonymity of investors.
• 	Monthly reporting of issuance of P-Notes
Can P-Notes be traded outside India? Yes. 	But only the among the
entities based in FATF member countries.
SEBI’s Guidelines (2024)
• 	FPIs issuing P-Notes shall have special type of registration with term
“ODI” as suffix.
• 	FPI cannot issue P-Notes against derivatives.

By: Basava Uppin	World Bank’s Catastrophe Bonds
Country X
Faces Disaster risk which
could lead to huge Economic
Loss
Pays Premium of Rs 1
crore/year for next 10 years
to get Insurance Cover of Rs
100 crores
Issue CAT Bonds
• 	Face Value: Rs 100 Crores ( Equal to Insurance
Cover)
• 	Maturity: 10 Years (Equal to Duration of Insurance)
• 	Coupon rate: Premium Paid + Returns on AAA
rated Assets
Money raised through CAT Bonds is invested in highly rated (AAA) assets such as US Treasury Bonds.
Returns generated on AAA assets along with Premium
paid by Country X is paid to Investors of CAT Bond
Scenario 1: No Disaster in Country X in next 10 years à CAT Bond Investors get
back Rs 100 crores.
Benefit for CAT Bond Investors: Get Back Principal Amount. Returns on CAT
Bonds higher than AAA rated Investments.
Scenario 2: Disaster in Country X in next 10 years à CAT Bond Investors lose
out 100 crores.
Benefit for country X: World Bank provides Insurance cover of Rs 100 crores.

question: Consider the following statements: Statement-I: The Tax Buoyancy in India has decreased to its lowest value since Covid-19. Statement-III: Income Tax exemptions to individuals and rationalization of Excise duty and Customs duty has led to decrease in percentage change in Tax revenue. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

context:
By: Basava Uppin	Trends in Deficits
Large scale increase in Fiscal
Deficit during the pandemic to
provide Fiscal Stimulus
Inability of Government to meet Fiscal
Deficit Target of 3% by end of March 2021
under FRBM Act, 2003
Commitment made by the Finance
Minister in the Budget Speech of
2021-22 to reduce FD to below
4.5% by FY 2025-26
Steady decline in RD, ERD and PD in
the last 5 years.
Government has been able to keep
the FD below 4.5% by FY 2025-26
New Line of Thinking on Fiscal Consolidation
• 	Shift from rigid annual FD Targets towards
more flexible FD Targets.
• 	Debt to GDP Ratio to be Fiscal Anchor and
FD to be the only Operational Target.
• 	Government 	would 	endeavour 	to 	keep
fiscal deficit in each year (from FY 2026-27
till 	FY 	2030-31) 	such 	that 	the 	Central
Government debt is on declining path to
attain a debt to GDP level of about 50±1
per cent by 31st March 2031

From where does Government borrow money to finance
deficit?

Note: Golden Fiscal Rule states that the entire Fiscal
Deficit should be used for financing capital expenditure

Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
(a) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
(b) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, but Statement II does not explain Statement I
(c) Statement I is correct, but Statement II is incorrect
(d) Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is correct

By: Basava Uppin
From where does Government borrow money to finance
deficit?
• 	Market Borrowings through Dated Secs, T-Bills and
CMBs. Among the Market Borrowings, Dated Secs
issued account for the highest share.
• 	Securities against Small Savings
• 	External Loans from Foreign Institutions
Note: Golden Fiscal Rule states that the entire Fiscal
Deficit should be used for financing capital expenditure
Sources of Deficit Financing
Prelims 2022
With reference to the Indian economy, consider the following
statements:
1. 	A share of the household financial savings goes towards
government borrowings.
2. 	Dated securities issued at market-related rates in auctions
form a large component of internal debt.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
(a) 1 only 	(b) 2 only 	(c) Both 1 and 2 	(d) Neither 1 nor 2

By: Basava Uppin
Mahila Samman Savings Certificate
• 	Introduced in Budget 2023-24. Eligible Beneficiaries: Women or Guardian of Minor Girl
• 	Minimum Deposits: Rs 1000. Maximum Deposits: Rs 2 Lakhs
• 	Interest Rate: 7.5%
• 	Maturity: 2 Years
• 	Can we open multiple accounts? Yes
• 	Tax Benefits: No Tax benefits as tax is to be paid on the interest earned from the deposits.
Indian Express, Mar 2025
Background: Prior to 2017, all the states were
allowed to borrow money from National Small
Savings Fund (NSSF).
14th Finance 	Commission 	Recommendations:
Borrowings by the states from NSSF is costly as
compared to Market Borrowings as the interest
rates are higher. Discontinue NSSF loans to the
states.
Cabinet Decision in 2017: 	Discontinue NSSF
Loans to the states. However, it was allowed
only for few states such as Arunachal Pradesh,
MP, Delhi, Kerala.

By: Basava Uppin	Highlights of the RBI’s Report on State Finances 2025-26
Major Source of Revenue for States: States’ own tax revenue followed by Central Transfers.
Major Source of Taxes for States: State GST, Sales Tax/VAT and Excise duty on Liquor.
Trends in GST Revenue:
• 	Aggregate revenue from taxes subsumed under GST has declined from 6.5% of GDP in
2015-16 to 5.5% in 2023-24.
• 	Most states collect less through GST (as % of GSDP) than earlier revenue from subsumed
taxes.
Categorization of Expenditure of States:
• 	Developmental Expenditure: Includes both Capital and Revenue expenditure on
• 	Infra development
• 	Social Services (Education, Health, Mid-Day Meals, Sanitation, Water Supply etc.)
• 	Welfare scheme for Poor and Vulnerable.
• 	Committed 	Expenditure: 	Includes 	Interest 	Payments, 	Salaries 	and 	Pensions 	and
Administrative expenses.
Trends in Expenditure:
• 	States spent more than 60% of the expenditure on committed expenditure leading to
constrain on developmental expenditure.
• 	Increase in Capital Expenditure by the states (despite share of states' expenditure on
capital expenditure remaining flat) due to scheme for financial assistance to the states for
Capital Expenditure.
Trends in Fiscal Deficit of States:
• 	Decrease in the Fiscal Deficit of the states from 4.1% (2020-21) to 3.3% in 2025-26.
• 	FD of the states is above the threshold level of 3% of GSDP.
States with the highest Fiscal Deficit: Bihar, Andhra Pradesh and Chhattisgarh
Financing of Fiscal Deficit: 75% of the Fiscal deficit of the states is financed through market
borrowings. Only 3 states and 1 UT avail loans from NSSF.
Trends in Debt of states:
• 	Consolidated debt of States declined to 28 per cent of GDP from a peak of 31 per cent at
end-March 2021.
• 	Only 3 states (Odisha, Maharashtra and Gujarat) have debt lower than threshold of 20%
fixed under the FRBM Act, 2003
States with the highest Debt: Arunachal Pradesh (57% of GSDP) and Punjab (47% of GSDP)
NITI Aayog’s Fiscal Health Index
• 	Top Performers: Odisha, Chhattisgarh and Goa
• 	Under-Performers: Punjab, WB and Kerala

subject: Economy
question: With respect to recent changes in the Buy Back tax introduced in Union Budget 2026-27, consider the following statements: 1. The burden of payment of Buyback tax has now been shifted from the companies to the shareholders. 2. The Buyback tax is to be paid in the form of capital gains tax by deducting the cost of acquisition. Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
context:
By: Basava Uppin
Under MAT Regime, Companies would be required to calculate
• 	Corporate Tax Liability under Income Tax Act and
• 	MAT Liability under Companies Act
Company would be required to pay either the corporate Tax or MAT, whichever is higher.
MAT Credit
If a company ends up paying tax under MAT instead of the normal tax, it is allowed to claim
MAT credit. This means the extra tax paid due to MAT can be carried forward and adjusted
against future tax liabilities when the company’s normal income tax becomes higher than
MAT.
Problem
42% of the Taxable corporate income still remains in the old tax regime
Budget 2026-27 Announcement
• 	MAT will be treated as Final Tax i.e.; companies cannot accumulate MAT Credit in future.
• 	MAT rate has been reduced from 15% to 14%

By: Basava Uppin	Foreign Asset of Small Taxpayers Disclosure Scheme, 2026
Present Status: Under the Income Tax act, Taxpayers are required to disclose their
foreign assets and income in their income tax returns. Failure to do so attracts
penalty under the Black money act.
Problem: Large number of small taxpayers have undisclosed foreign income and
assets in the form of Dormant Bank accounts, Savings and Insurance Policies, Social
security accumulation. Need to provide immunity to such small taxpayers from
prosecution.
Mandate: By declaring undisclosed foreign income and assets, taxpayers would pay
required tax and fee and 	hence would enjoy legal prosecution under the Black
Money Act, 2015.
Applicability: Residents and Non-Residents (Provided they were Residents before).
Tax Rate:
• 	Undisclosed Foreign Asset/Incomeà If it is less than Rs 1 croreà Tax Rate
(30%) + Penalty (30%)
• 	Paid Tax on foreign income but did not declare the asset acquired from the
incomeà If the value of acquired asset is Rs 5 croresà Flat rate of Rs 1 lakhs.
Safe Harbour Rules for IT Companies
Meaning: Safe Harbour rules are the rules in the Income Tax act wherein the tax
authorities accept the transfer price quoted by the taxpayer provided certain
conditions are fulfilled.
Need for Safe Harbour rules: Certainty to taxpayers + Reduce Tax litigations
Conditions to be met for availing Safe Harbour rules
• 	Turnover should be below certain threshold level.
• 	Profit margin of the company should be below certain threshold level
Budget Announcement:
• 	Turnover limit for the IT companies to avail Safe Harbour has been increased
from Rs 300 crores to Rs 2000 crores.
• 	Earlier, the profit margin for IT companies was different for different services.
Now, the profit margin would be 15.5% for all services.

By: Basava UppinOne Time Exemption to SEZs
Prelims Pointers on SEZs
What are SEZs? Duty-free enclave and deemed to be a foreign territory for the
purposes of trade operations and taxes. All the territory outside the SEZ is the
Domestic Tariff Area (DTA). SEZs can be set up by the Centre, State or a private
entity.
Incentives for the SEZ Units:
• 	Duty free import of Goods
• 	100% Income Tax exemption on export income for SEZ units for first 5 years, 50%
for next 5 years thereafter and 50% of the ploughed back export profit for next 5
years.
• 	Supplies to SEZs are zero rated under IGST Act, 2017.
Supply of Goods and services by SEZ units to DTA are treated as imports into India.
Minimum alternate Tax (MAT) has been extended to SEZ Units in 2011.
Introduction of sunset clause on Tax Benefits (2017): SEZ Units would enjoy tax
holiday 	for 15 years. However, this

• 	Corporate Tax Liability under Income Tax Act and
• 	MAT Liability under Companies Act
Company would be required to pay either the corporate Tax or MAT, whichever is higher.
MAT Credit
If a company ends up paying tax under MAT instead of the normal tax, it is allowed to claim
MAT credit. This means the extra tax paid due to MAT can be carried forward and adjusted
against future tax liabilities when the company’s normal income tax becomes higher than
MAT.
Problem
42% of the Taxable corporate income still remains in the old tax regime
Budget 2026-27 Announcement
• 	MAT will be treated as Final Tax i.e.; companies cannot accumulate MAT Credit in future.
• 	MAT rate has been reduced from 15% to 14%

By: Basava Uppin	Foreign Asset of Small Taxpayers Disclosure Scheme, 2026
Present Status: Under the Income Tax act, Taxpayers are required to disclose their
foreign assets and income in their income tax returns. Failure to do so attracts
penalty under the Black money act.
Problem: Large number of small taxpayers have undisclosed foreign income and
assets in the form of Dormant Bank accounts, Savings and Insurance Policies, Social
security accumulation. Need to provide immunity to such small taxpayers from
prosecution.
Mandate: By declaring undisclosed foreign income and assets, taxpayers would pay
required tax and fee and 	hence would enjoy legal prosecution under the Black
Money Act, 2015.
Applicability: Residents and Non-Residents (Provided they were Residents before).
Tax Rate:
• 	Undisclosed Foreign Asset/Incomeà If it is less than Rs 1 croreà Tax Rate
(30%) + Penalty (30%)
• 	Paid Tax on foreign income but did not declare the asset acquired from the
incomeà If the value of acquired asset is Rs 5 croresà Flat rate of Rs 1 lakhs.
Safe Harbour Rules for IT Companies
Meaning: Safe Harbour rules are the rules in the Income Tax act wherein the tax
authorities accept the transfer price quoted by the taxpayer provided certain
conditions are fulfilled.
Need for Safe Harbour rules: Certainty to taxpayers + Reduce Tax litigations
Conditions to be met for availing Safe Harbour rules
• 	Turnover should be below certain threshold level.
• 	Profit margin of the company should be below certain threshold level
Budget Announcement:
• 	Turnover limit for the IT companies to avail Safe Harbour has been increased
from Rs 300 crores to Rs 2000 crores.
• 	Earlier, the profit margin for IT companies was different for different services.
Now, the profit margin would be 15.5% for all services.

By: Basava UppinOne Time Exemption to SEZs
Prelims Pointers on SEZs
What are SEZs? Duty-free enclave and deemed to be a foreign territory for the
purposes of trade operations and taxes. All the territory outside the SEZ is the
Domestic Tariff Area (DTA). SEZs can be set up by the Centre, State or a private
entity.
Incentives for the SEZ Units:
• 	Duty free import of Goods
• 	100% Income Tax exemption on export income for SEZ units for first 5 years, 50%
for next 5 years thereafter and 50% of the ploughed back export profit for next 5
years.
• 	Supplies to SEZs are zero rated under IGST Act, 2017.
Supply of Goods and services by SEZ units to DTA are treated as imports into India.
Minimum alternate Tax (MAT) has been extended to SEZ Units in 2011.
Introduction of sunset clause on Tax Benefits (2017): SEZ Units would enjoy tax
holiday 	for 15 years. However, this

subject: Economy
question: Which among the following can be considered as External Commercial Borrowings (ECBs) in India? 1. An Individual resident borrowing loans from foreign Bank 2. A Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) company issuing Bonds in USA 3. An Indian Resident company issuing Foreign currency denominated Bond in IFSC 4. An Indian Resident company issuing Masala Bonds in UK. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
context:
By: Basava Uppin
• 	Countries attracting highest FDI: USA ($ 250 bn); China and Singapore.
India- placed at 8th Position)
• 	Trends: Gross FDI inflows has been fluctuating. India received its highest
ever FDI inflows of around $ 85 bn in 2021-22.
• 	Top FDI Sources: Singapore and Mauritius.
• 	Sectors 	attracting 	highest 	FDI: 	Services 	sector 	(Banking, 	Insurance)
followed by Computer Software.
• 	States attracting highest FDI Inflows: Maharashtra and Karnataka.
Difference between Gross and Net FDI Inflows
Net FDI = Gross FDI – (Repatriation of Profits + Overseas Direct Investment)
Trends in Gross and Net FDI Inflows
• 	Gross FDI inflows has been fluctuating. India received its highest ever FDI inflows
of around $ 85 bn in 2021-22.
• 	Net FDI reduced by 96% in 2024-25 as compared to previous financial year.
Further, Net FDI has remained negative for fifth straight month in Jan 2026.
Reasons for fall in Net FDI
• 	Increase in Overseas Direct Investment. Key Destinations: Singapore, US and UAE
accounting for more than 50% of FDI.
• 	Increase in Repatriation of profits by the companies: Returns on FDI = (Direct
Investment Income/Inward FDI Stock). India’s average return on FDI is more than
the emerging economies.
Details about Overseas Direct Investment
Legal Framework: The overseas investment by a person resident in India is governed
by Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999
Regulated by RBI
Meaning:
• 	Unlisted foreign company
• 	Investment of 10% or more in shares of foreign company
• 	Investment of less than 10% shares of foreign company, but accompanied by
management control in foreign company.
Automatic Route: No prior approval of RBI for investments up to $ 1 billion in a year.
Approval Route: Prior approval of RBI for investments of more than $ 1 billion in a
year.
Note: The Investment made in International Financial Services Centre (IFSC) by Indian
Resident is considered as Overseas Investment

By: Basava Uppin
BS: Feb 2026
RBI’s Revised ECB Framework
Meaning of ECBs: Loans raised by residents from Non-Residents. Governed
under FEMA, 1999
Eligible Borrowers: Any entity resident in India (other than an individual)
that is registered under a Central or State Act
Recognised lenders:
• 	A person resident outside India;
• 	A branch outside India of an entity whose lending business is regulated
by the Reserve Bank; and
• 	A financial institution or a branch of a financial institution set up in IFSC.
Currency 	of 	Borrowing: 	Either 	Rupees 	(Ex: 	Masala 	Bonds) 	or 	Foreign
Currency
Forms of Borrowings:
• 	Loans
• 	Bonds
• 	Trade Credits (beyond 3 years)
Borrowing limits: up to $ 1 billion or 300% of the net worth (whichever is
higher)
Minimum maturity period of ECBs: 3 years

Mint: Feb 2026
Meaning: All resident individuals, including minors, are allowed to freely remit up to USD
2,50,000 per financial year (April – March) for any permissible current or capital account
transaction or a combination of both.
Note: The scheme is not applicable to corporates, partnership firms, HUF, Trusts etc.
Purpose for which money can be remitted?: Any permissible current and capital account
transactions. For example, residents can invest in listed foreign shares, bonds, mutual
funds, VCFs, real estate projects etc.
Prohibited items:
• 	Purchase of lottery tickets
• 	Trading in foreign exchange
• 	Trading in Derivatives listed on Foreign Exchange
Are there any restrictions on the kind/quality of debt or equity instruments an individual
can invest in?: No
Can Investments be made in IFSC through LRS? Yes

subject: Economy
question: Consider the following statements: Statement-I: The share of Dollar in the global Forex Reserves has declined gradually in the last 2 decades. Statement-II: Central Banks across the world have been diversifying their forex reserves to reduce dependence on Dollars. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct explanation for Statement- I
options.b: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is not the correct explanation for Statement-I
options.c: Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
options.d: Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct

context:
By: Basava Uppin
Intervention by the Central Bank in Fixing Exchange rate increases
Role of the Market Forces of Demand and Supply in Fixing Exchange rate increases
Floating Exchange Rate 	Soft Pegged Exchange Rate 	Hard Pegged Exchange Rate
Exchange 	Arrangements 	with 	No
Separate Legal Tender: Currency of
another country circulates as sole
legal tender (Formal Dollarization)
Ex: Ecuador, Bahamas, Argentina
Currency Board Arrangements:
1. 	Fixed Exchange Rate
2. 	Issue 	of 	Domestic 	Currency
against 	equivalent 	amount 	of
Foreign Currency
Ex: Hong Kong Central Bank issues
Hong 	Kong 	Dollars 	against
equivalent 	US 	Dollars 	kept 	in 	its
reserves
Conventional Pegged System: Country pegs its domestic currency
to another currency or a basket of currencies. Exchange rate is
fixed.
• 	Saudi Rial is pegged to US Dollars at $1 = 3.75 Rial since 1986.
• 	Kuwaiti Dinar is pegged to a basket of currencies.
• 	Nepali Rupee is pegged to Indian Rupee at 1 Rupee = 1.6
Nepali Rupee (1994)
• 	Bhutanese Ngultrum is pegged to Indian Rupee at 1 Rupee = 1
Bhutanese Ngultrum.
Stabilized 	Arrangement: 	Exchange 	rate 	that 	remains 	within 	a
margin of 	2% for 	six months or 	more due to Intervention of
Central Bank.
The country does not notify the margin to the Public and IMF.
Crawling 	Peg: 	Exchange 	rate 	that 	remains 	within 	a 	narrow
margin. The Country notifies the margin to the Public and IMF.
Exchange 	rate 	is 	fixed 	due 	to
market 	forces 	of 	Demand 	and
Supply.
Intervention by the Central Bank
takes 	place 	only 	in 	exceptional
circumstances.
Ex: USA
BS: Nov 2025

By: Basava Uppin
Meaning
Assets which are readily available to the RBI for
(a) 	financing BoP (Meeting shortfall in BoP)
(b) 	Intervention in the forex market to check exchange rate volatility
(i.e., to sell Dollars to check Rupee Depreciation)
How does RBI Build Forex Reserves?
In event of surplus in the BoP, the RBI purchases the surplus dollars from
the market (Authorized Dealers) and builds forex reserves.
Legal Framework
RBI Act, 1934 empowers the RBI to act as Custodian of Forex Reserves.
Components of Forex Reserves
Foreign Exchange Assets: Foreign Currency, Foreign G-Secs, Deposits with
other Central Banks, Deposits with Overseas Branches of Commercial
Banks
Monetary Gold: Gold with the RBI as part of Forex Reserves
Special Drawing Rights (SDRs): International Reserve Asset of the IMF
Reserve Position in IMF: Part of IMF’s Quota which is part of Forex
Reserves
Note: The FCAs and Gold with the Issue Department of RBI is also part of
Forex Reserves
Countries with Highest Forex Reserves
1. 	China ($ 3.3 Trillion)
2. 	Japan ($1.3 Trillion)
3. 	Switzerland ($900 bn)
4. 	India ($ 799 bn)
Countries with Highest Gold Reserves
USA, Germany, Italy, France, Russia
Adequacy of Reserves
Import Cover: 	Number of months of imports
that could be paid for by 	Forex reserves. In
India, it is presently around 8-9 months.
Greenspan-Guidotti 	rule: 	Forex 	Reserves
should be sufficient to pay the short-term
External Debt.
Ratio of Reserves to Broad Money: Captures
the risk of Bank run. Should be above 20%.
Ratio 	of 	Reserves 	to 	Financial 	outflows:
Captures the risk of Capital Flight.
Forex 	Reserves 	to 	GDP 	Ratio: 	Denotes
Economic resilience.
Largest Share
Lowest Share
ET, Dec 2025 Mint, Oct 2025
Important Developments in Global Forex Reserves
• 	Dollar accounts for the largest share of Global Forex Reserves.
• 	Decline in share of Dollar in the last 2 decades from 70% to 60%.
• 	De-Dollarization Efforts
• 	Diversification of Risks away from USA
• 	US Sanctions on Russia and Iran
• 	Decline in share of Dollar is not accompanied by increase in
share of other Big 4 currencies- Euro, Pound, Yen
• 	Decline in share of Dollar is accompanied by increase in share of
Non-Traditional Currencies such as Chinese Renminbi, Australian
Dollar, Canadian Dollar etc.
• 	Increase in share of Gold Holdings in Global Forex Reserves.

question: Which among the following indicators are used to measure Adequacy of Forex Reserves in a country? 1. Import Cover 2. Ratio of Forex Reserves to Short-Term Debt 3. Ratio of Forex Reserves to FPI outflows 4. Ratio of Forex Reserves to Broad Money Select the correct answer using the code given below:
context:
By: Basava Uppin
Intervention by the Central Bank in Fixing Exchange rate increases
Role of the Market Forces of Demand and Supply in Fixing Exchange rate increases
Floating Exchange Rate 	Soft Pegged Exchange Rate 	Hard Pegged Exchange Rate
Exchange 	Arrangements 	with 	No
Separate Legal Tender: Currency of
another country circulates as sole
legal tender (Formal Dollarization)
Ex: Ecuador, Bahamas, Argentina
Currency Board Arrangements:
1. 	Fixed Exchange Rate
2. 	Issue 	of 	Domestic 	Currency
against 	equivalent 	amount 	of
Foreign Currency
Ex: Hong Kong Central Bank issues
Hong 	Kong 	Dollars 	against
equivalent 	US 	Dollars 	kept 	in 	its
reserves
Conventional Pegged System: Country pegs its domestic currency
to another currency or a basket of currencies. Exchange rate is
fixed.
• 	Saudi Rial is pegged to US Dollars at $1 = 3.75 Rial since 1986.
• 	Kuwaiti Dinar is pegged to a basket of currencies.
• 	Nepali Rupee is pegged to Indian Rupee at 1 Rupee = 1.6
Nepali Rupee (1994)
• 	Bhutanese Ngultrum is pegged to Indian Rupee at 1 Rupee = 1
Bhutanese Ngultrum.
Stabilized 	Arrangement: 	Exchange 	rate 	that 	remains 	within 	a
margin of 	2% for 	six months or 	more due to Intervention of
Central Bank.
The country does not notify the margin to the Public and IMF.
Crawling 	Peg: 	Exchange 	rate 	that 	remains 	within 	a 	narrow
margin. The Country notifies the margin to the Public and IMF.
Exchange 	rate 	is 	fixed 	due 	to
market 	forces 	of 	Demand 	and
Supply.
Intervention by the Central Bank
takes 	place 	only 	in 	exceptional
circumstances.
Ex: USA
BS: Nov 2025

By: Basava Uppin
Meaning
Assets which are readily available to the RBI for
(a) 	financing BoP (Meeting shortfall in BoP)
(b) 	Intervention in the forex market to check exchange rate volatility
(i.e., to sell Dollars to check Rupee Depreciation)
How does RBI Build Forex Reserves?
In event of surplus in the BoP, the RBI purchases the surplus dollars from
the market (Authorized Dealers) and builds forex reserves.
Legal Framework
RBI Act, 1934 empowers the RBI to act as Custodian of Forex Reserves.
Components of Forex Reserves
Foreign Exchange Assets: Foreign Currency, Foreign G-Secs, Deposits with
other Central Banks, Deposits with Overseas Branches of Commercial
Banks
Monetary Gold: Gold with the RBI as part of Forex Reserves
Special Drawing Rights (SDRs): International Reserve Asset of the IMF
Reserve Position in IMF: Part of IMF’s Quota which is part of Forex
Reserves
Note: The FCAs and Gold with the Issue Department of RBI is also part of
Forex Reserves
Countries with Highest Forex Reserves
1. 	China ($ 3.3 Trillion)
2. 	Japan ($1.3 Trillion)
3. 	Switzerland ($900 bn)
4. 	India ($ 799 bn)
Countries with Highest Gold Reserves
USA, Germany, Italy, France, Russia
Adequacy of Reserves
Import Cover: 	Number of months of imports
that could be paid for by 	Forex reserves. In
India, it is presently around 8-9 months.
Greenspan-Guidotti 	rule: 	Forex 	Reserves
should be sufficient to pay the short-term
External Debt.
Ratio of Reserves to Broad Money: Captures
the risk of Bank run. Should be above 20%.
Ratio 	of 	Reserves 	to 	Financial 	outflows:
Captures the risk of Capital Flight.
Forex 	Reserves 	to 	GDP 	Ratio: 	Denotes
Economic resilience.
Largest Share
Lowest Share
ET, Dec 2025 Mint, Oct 2025
Important Developments in Global Forex Reserves
• 	Dollar accounts for the largest share of Global Forex Reserves.
• 	Decline in share of Dollar in the last 2 decades from 70% to 60%.
• 	De-Dollarization Efforts
• 	Diversification of Risks away from USA
• 	US Sanctions on Russia and Iran
• 	Decline in share of Dollar is not accompanied by increase in
share of other Big 4 currencies- Euro, Pound, Yen
• 	Decline in share of Dollar is accompanied by increase in share of
Non-Traditional Currencies such as Chinese Renminbi, Australian
Dollar, Canadian Dollar etc.
• 	Increase in share of Gold Holdings in Global Forex Reserves.

By: Basava Uppin	Major Developments in WTO
E-Commerce Moratorium
Meaning of E-Commerce: Delivery of goods and services (such as Films, music, video games,
software, newspapers, books etc.) by electronic means.
Present Status:
• 	In 1998, the WTO members came out with a Declaration on global Electronic Commerce,
under which countries have decided not to impose customs duties on E-Commerce
transactions.
• 	This moratorium is renewed by the members at every Ministerial Conference, which
usually happens every two years.
Present Debate over Moratorium on Customs duties on E-Commerce
• 	Developed countries are the major exporters of these digital goods and services. Hence,
the developed countries are in favour of making this moratorium on e-commerce
transactions permanent.
• 	However, it has been opposed by developing countries such as India due to loss of
revenue
Special and Differential Treatment
Meaning: Provide for special privileges and benefits for LDCs and Developing Economies
• 	Extra time to fulfil their commitments in many of the WTO agreement

• 	In 1998, the WTO members came out with a Declaration on global Electronic Commerce,
under which countries have decided not to impose customs duties on E-Commerce
transactions.
• 	This moratorium is renewed by the members at every Ministerial Conference, which
usually happens every two years.
Present Debate over Moratorium on Customs duties on E-Commerce
• 	Developed countries are the major exporters of these digital goods and services. Hence,
the developed countries are in favour of making this moratorium on e-commerce
transactions permanent.
• 	However, it has been opposed by developing countries such as India due to loss of
revenue
Special and Differential Treatment
Meaning: Provide for special privileges and benefits for LDCs and Developing Economies
• 	Extra time to fulfil their commitments in many of the WTO agreement

• 	Developed countries are the major exporters of these digital goods and services. Hence,

subject: Economy
question: With respect to WTO Agreement on Fisheries, consider the following statements: 1. The WTO Agreement on Fisheries is the first agreement under WTO to focus on environment. 2. This agreement seeks to prohibit all the fishing subsidies given by the member countries 3. This agreement has incorporated special and differential treatment for the benefit of LDCs and developing countries. 4. The agreement has entered into force in 2025 after being ratified by 2/3 of member countries. Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
context:
By: Basava UppinJoint Initiative on Services Domestic Regulation:
Present Status: Under the General Agreement on Trade in Services (GATS), the WTO member
countries are allowed to determine the extent of market access they will grant to foreign
service providers. The foreign service providers must seek authorisations or licenses from
designated authorities to operate in the host country.
Problem: However, processes for applying and obtaining the licenses are not clear in many
countries.
How Services Domestic Regulations (SDR) solves the problem? The SDRs are aimed at making
it easier for foreign service providers to access, understand and follow the procedures for
getting authorisations or licenses for operating in the host country.
Dispute Redressal Mechanism under WTO
Understanding Dispute Settlement Mechanism under WTO
Settling disputes is the responsibility of the Dispute Settlement Body which consists of
all WTO members.
• 	First stage: consultation (up to 60 days). If not resolved, then next stage.
• 	Second Stage: Complaining country can ask for a panel to be appointed. The report
becomes the Dispute Settlement Body’s ruling unless a consensus rejects it. This
entire process should be completed within 1 year.
• 	Appeal Stage: Permanent seven-member Appellate Body set up by the Dispute
Settlement Body and broadly representing the range of WTO membership. Members
of the Appellate Body have four-year terms
Present Problem:
• 	Sanctioned strength of the Appellate Body (AB) is seven members, and these
members are appointed through consensus among the member countries.
• 	Quorum required to decide on disputes is 3 judges.
• 	US has so far been blocking appointment of members to the Appellate Body (AB) and
hence AB does not have required strength of judges.
Multi-Party Interim Appeal Arbitration Arrangement(MPIA):
In the absence of a functioning WTO Appellate body, MPIA acts an interim, alternative
mechanism for resolving WTO disputes.
Participating members can resolve disputes by using arbitrations. The MPIA is likely to
remain in place for as long as the WTO Appellate Body is not functioning and hence it
serves as a temporary solution.
Note: India has been opposed to MPIA as it is an informal dispute settlement
mechanism and hence undermines multilateralism
Agreement on Fisheries Subsidies:
Background: In June 2022, WTO adopted a binding multilateral Agreement on Fisheries
Subsidies in line with UN SDGs on eliminating unsustainable fisheries subsidies.
Achievement: First SDG target to be met through a multilateral agreement; First WTO
agreement to focus on the environment; and Only the second agreement reached at the
WTO after the Trade Facilitation Agreement in 2013.
Main features of agreement:
• 	Legally binding agreement
• 	Prohibit subsidies for
• 	Illegal fishing.
• 	Unreported fishing
• 	Unregulated fishing
• 	Negotiations underway for reducing subsidies for overcapacity and overfishing.
• 	Targeted technical assistance and capacity building to developing Members and LDCs
through voluntarily funded WTO Fisheries Funding Mechanism.
• 	Special and Differential treatment: Subsidies of developing and LDC Members benefit
from a peace clause under which dispute settlement will not apply for two years from
the date of entry into force of the Agreement.
Recent development: Agreement entered into force in Sep 2025 after being ratified by two-
third of the member countries.
WTO reforms
Decision making under WTO:
Ministerial Conference, comprising of representatives of all the member countries the
highest policy making body. It meets at least once every two years. The decisions of the
Ministerial conference is based upon consensus.
Present Debate: U.S. and EU argue consensus makes rule-making slow and ineffective.
They advocate plurilateral agreements among smaller groups.
India defends consensus as the foundation of a fair multilateral system. It believes
consensus ensures equal voice for developing countries and prevents dominance by major
economies.

question: Consider the following statements regarding National Biofuels Policy, 2018: 1. The Policy targets 20% Ethanol blending for Petrol and Diesel by 2030. 2. Only Food grains unfit for human consumption can be used as Feedstock. 3. The prices of Ethanol is fixed by the Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs (CCEA) Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
context:
By: Basava Uppin	Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana
Mandate 	Provide for Crop Insurance to farmers to cover for their crop losses
Coverage 	• 	Land owning Farmers, Sharecroppers and Tenant farmers
Risks covered 	• 	Prevented 	sowing: 	Coverage 	if 	the 	insured 	area 	is 	prevented 	from
sowing/planting due to adverse weather conditions.
• 	Yield losses due to Natural Calamities, Pests and Diseases
• 	Post-harvest losses
• 	Localized Calamities such as Paddy inundation in Flood prone and coastal
areas.
• 	State Governments have flexibility to include attack by wild animals (Rules
notified in Nov 2025)
Note: Losses due to war and Nuclear attack not covered.
Premium paid by
Farmers
Kharif: 2%; Rabi: 1.5%; Commercial Crops: 5%
Remaining balance is to be paid by Centre and States.
Note: It is not mandatory for loanee farmers to take insurance.
Limit on Centre’s
Premium
• 	Centre would contribute the premium amount provided the premium is up
to 30% for unirrigated areas/crops and 25% for irrigated areas/crops.
• 	If the premium is above the threshold, then the Centre would not provide
the additional premium amount. So, in that case, the additional premium
amount would be borne by the respective state government.
E-NAM
Details 	Pan-India Electronic portal to link the existing APMCs to create an Integrated domestic
market
Condition on
States to Join E-
NAM
States interested to integrate their mandis with E-NAM are required to carry out
following three reforms in their APMC Act.
a) Single trading license (Unified) to be valid across the state
b) Single point levy of market fee across the state; and
c) Provision for e-auction/ e-trading as a mode of price discovery
Managed by 	Small Farmers' Agribusiness Consortium (SFAC).
Recent
Developments
FPO Module on e-NAM: Enables FPOs to sell their produce directly from collection
centres without the need to come to Mandis.
Warehousing based Trading Module: Farmers can sell their produce directly from the
warehouses registered under warehousing Development and Regulating Authority
(WDRA).
Logistics Module: 	Link large logistic aggregator platforms with the traders for the
seamless transportation of Agri-produce.
Credit Guarantee Scheme for Negotiable warehousing receipts-based Pledge
Financing
Details about Negotiable Warehousing receipts (NWRs)
Legal Framework: Warehousing development and Regulation Act (WDR Act)
provides for issuance of NWRs by the warehouses registered under Act.
Regulation by Warehousing Development and Regulatory Authority. It functions
under the Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food and Public Distribution.
Benefits of Negotiable Warehousing Receipts for the farmers: Store perishable
commodities + Use collateral for short-term borrowing + direct sale of the
commodities under new farm acts + Scientific storage + Reduce post-harvest
losses.
How is the Scheme beneficial for farmers?
Farmers store their produce in warehouses registered with the Warehousing
Development Regulatory Authority (WDRA) and avail loan against e-NWRs.
Banks are given guarantee on repayment of loans by farmers. The guarantee is
provided by Credit Guarantee Trust for e-NWR.
Dalhan Atmanirbharta Mission
Important Facts about Pulses:
• 	India’s Status at Global Level: 25% of Global production, 38% of Global area,
27% of global consumption and 14% of global import of Pulses
• 	Area and Production of Pulses (percentage): 23% of the area under Food
grains and 10% to the food grain production in country.
• 	Area and Production of Pulses (Absolute value): 25 MT (Production) and 27.5
Mha (Area)
• 	Import Burden of Pulses: Almost 20% of the Domestic requirements met
through imports in 2023-24.
Target under Mission:
• 	Increase area under Pulses from 27.5 Mha to 31 Mha
• 	Increase production from 25 MT to 35 MT
• 	Increase yield from 880 kg/ha to 1130 kg/ha
Strategy:
• 	Developing high-yielding, climate resilient and pest resistant varieties of pulses.
• 	100% Procurement of Tur, Urad and Masur under Price Support Scheme
• 	Duration: 6 years (2025-2031)

By: Basava Uppin	National Policy on Biofuels
Mandate: Blending of Ethanol with Petrol to reduce import of crude oil,
reduce emissions and ensure higher prices to farmers.
Target:
• 	20% Blending in Petrol by 2025-26
• 	5% Blending of Bio-diesel in Diesel by 2030. However, currently there is
no blending of Ethanol with Diesel.
Eligible raw materials:
• 	Sugarcane-based raw materials - Molasses, sugarcane juice / sugar etc.
• 	Surplus rice and Maize with Food Corporation of India (FCI)
• 	Food grains unfit for human consumption
Administered Pricing Mechanism (APM) for Ethanol:
• 	Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs fixes the price of Ethanol
• 	Price of Ethanol is based upon the underlying feedstock and hence it
depends on the raw material used.
• 	Oil Marketing companies purchase Ethanol from the Industries at the
price fixed by the Government.
Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas.

It uses drone technology for the mapping of property. 3. It is a central sector scheme implemented by the Ministry of Rural Development. Which among the statements given above is/are correct?

By: Basava Uppin
Important facts about Cooperatives
• 	Number of Cooperatives: 8.44 lakh cooperatives
• 	Membership: 30+ crore members covering 99% of villages and
71% of rural households.
• 	Types of Cooperatives-
• 	2 Lakh Credit Cooperatives (PACS, UCBs etc.)
• 	6 lakh Non-Credit Cooperatives (Dairy, Marketing, Housing
etc.)
• 	World’s largest Cooperatives in terms of Turnover per capita GDP:
AMUL and IFFCO. Note: In terms of absolute Turnover, these two
are not the largest Cooperatives.
• 	UN International Year of Cooperatives 2025
• 	Adopted by UN through a resolution under the Sponsorship
of Mongolia and Kenya.
• 	Previously, 2012 was celebrated as International Year of
Cooperatives. (This is not the first such celebration)
• 	Theme: “Cooperatives Build a better world”.
• 	International Cooperative Alliance
• 	Establishment: 1895
• 	Mandate: Apex body representing all cooperatives
• 	Membership: 	More 	than 	300 	organisations 	from 	105
countries. Represented by more than 1 billion members.
• 	Role: Permanent seat and a consultative status with the
United Nations Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC) and
is a founding member of the Committee for the Promotion
and Advancement of Cooperatives (COPAC).
• 	Headquarters: Brussels, Belgium
August 2025 	Nov 2025	July 2025
Important highlights of National Cooperation Policy
Background: The Cooperative policy 2002, was outdated due to the radical shifts brought on
by globalization, digitization, and socio-economic transformation.
Vision of New Policy: Achieve the Vision of Viksit Bharat through ”Sahakaar se Samriddhi”.
Objectives: To be achieved through the following 16 objectives over the next 10 years, grouped
under six strategic mission pillars:
Important Steps taken by Government to promote Cooperatives
Economic Strengthening of PACS
• 	Model Bye-laws which enable PACS to take up more than 25 different economic activities-
Seeds, Fertilisers, Custom hiring centres, Procurement, Dairy Farming, Fisheries, PDS,
Common Service centres.
• 	Priority to PACS for new Petrol/Diesel dealerships, Jan Aushadhi Kendras
• 	Computerisation of 67,000 functional PACS
• 	World's largest grain storage plan in the cooperative sector
3 New Multi-state Cooperative societies at National Level
• 	National Cooperative Exports Limited (NCEL)
• 	Bhartiya Beej Sahakari Samiti Limited (BBSSL)
• 	National Cooperative Organics Limited (NCOL)
Umbrella Organization for Urban Cooperative Banks.
National Cooperative University "Tribhuvan" to ensure quality supply of manpower.
Relief in Income Tax for Cooperative Societies
• 	Reduction in Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) rate for cooperative societies from 18.5% to 15%
• 	Reduction in Surcharge on Income Tax for Cooperative Societies
Legal Framework: Multi-State Cooperative Societies (MSCS) (Amendment) Act,2023 established
Cooperative Election Authority, Ombudsman at National Level and improved transparency and
accountability.
GeM Platform: Cooperative societies to be allowed to register as buyers on the GeM platform.

Important highlights of National Cooperation Policy
Background: The Cooperative policy 2002, was outdated due to the radical shifts brought on
by globalization, digitization, and socio-economic transformation.
Vision of New Policy: Achieve the Vision of Viksit Bharat through ”Sahakaar se Samriddhi”.
Objectives: To be achieved through the following 16 objectives over the next 10 years, grouped
under six strategic mission pillars:
Important Steps taken by Government to promote Cooperatives
Economic Strengthening of PACS
• 	Model Bye-laws which enable PACS to take up more than 25 different economic activities-
Seeds, Fertilisers, Custom hiring centres, Procurement, Dairy Farming, Fisheries, PDS,
Common Service centres.
• 	Priority to PACS for new Petrol/Diesel dealerships, Jan Aushadhi Kendras
• 	Computerisation of 67,000 functional PACS
• 	World's largest grain storage plan in the cooperative sector
3 New Multi-state Cooperative societies at National Level
• 	National Cooperative Exports Limited (NCEL)
• 	Bhartiya Beej Sahakari Samiti Limited (BBSSL)
• 	National Cooperative Organics Limited (NCOL)
Umbrella Organization for Urban Cooperative Banks.
National Cooperative University "Tribhuvan" to ensure quality supply of manpower.
Relief in Income Tax for Cooperative Societies
• 	Reduction in Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) rate for cooperative societies from 18.5% to 15%
• 	Reduction in Surcharge on Income Tax for Cooperative Societies
Legal Framework: Multi-State Cooperative Societies (MSCS) (Amendment) Act,2023 established
Cooperative Election Authority, Ombudsman at National Level and improved transparency and
accountability.
GeM Platform: Cooperative societies to be allowed to register as buyers on the GeM platform.

Mint, May 2023
Prelims 2011
With 	reference 	to 	micro-irrigation 	which 	of 	the 	following
statements is/are correct ?
1. 	Fertilizer/nutrient loss can be reduced.
2. 	It is the only means of irrigation in dry land farming.
3. 	In some areas of farming receding of ground water table can
be checked.
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
(a) 1 only 	(b) 2 and 3 only 	(c) 1 and 3 only 	(d) 1 2 and 3
Prelims 2016
Which of the following is/are the advantage /advantages of
practising drip irrigation?
1. 	Reduction in weed
2. 	Reduction in soil salinity
3. 	Reduction in soil erosion
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
(a) 1 and 2 only (b) 3 only 	(c) 1 and 3 only (d) None
Basics to understand
• 	Net Irrigated Area: Total area of land irrigated at least once during an agricultural year. Counts
land only once, even if irrigated multiple times.
• 	Gross Irrigated Area: Total area of land irrigated (including repeated irrigations on same land)
• 	Irrigation Intensity= (Gross Irrigated Area/ Net Irrigated Area)
Trends in Area under Irrigation
• 	Increase in both Net Irrigated area and Gross Irrigated area since 2016 to more than 50%.
• 	Net Irrigated Area= 78 mha (55% of Net Sown Area i.e., 141 mha)
• 	Gross Irrigated Area = 121 mha (55% of Gross Cropped area i.e., 220 mha)
• 	Irrigation Intensity has increased to 155%.
Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY)
Objectives:
• 	Achieve 	convergence 	of 	investments 	in
irrigation at the field level
• 	Expand 	cultivable 	area 	under 	assured
irrigation
• 	Improve on-farm water use efficiency
• 	Enhance the adoption of micro-Irrigation
Components and implementing Ministries
• 	Per drop, More Crop: Ministry of Agriculture
• 	Har Khet ko Paani: Ministry of Jal Shakti
• 	Accelerated 	Irrigation 	Benefit 	Programme
(AIBP): Ministry of Jal Shakti
• 	Watershed 	Development 	Programme:
Ministry of Rural Development
Subsidy under the scheme
55 per cent financial assistance to small and
marginal 	farmers 	and 	45 	per 	cent 	to 	other
farmers for the installation of Drip and Sprinkler
systems

(a) 	To achieve convergence of investment in irrigation at the
field level

(c) 	To improve on-farm water use efficiency to reduce wastage
of water
(d) 	To 	protect 	farmers 	against 	crop 	failure 	due 	to 	natural
calamities

3. 	In some areas of farming receding of ground water table can
be checked.
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
(a) 1 only 	(b) 2 and 3 only 	(c) 1 and 3 only 	(d) 1 2 and 3
Prelims 2016
Which of the following is/are the advantage /advantages of
practising drip irrigation?
1. 	Reduction in weed
2. 	Reduction in soil salinity
3. 	Reduction in soil erosion
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
(a) 1 and 2 only (b) 3 only 	(c) 1 and 3 only (d) None
Basics to understand
• 	Net Irrigated Area: Total area of land irrigated at least once during an agricultural year. Counts
land only once, even if irrigated multiple times.
• 	Gross Irrigated Area: Total area of land irrigated (including repeated irrigations on same land)
• 	Irrigation Intensity= (Gross Irrigated Area/ Net Irrigated Area)
Trends in Area under Irrigation
• 	Increase in both Net Irrigated area and Gross Irrigated area since 2016 to more than 50%.
• 	Net Irrigated Area= 78 mha (55% of Net Sown Area i.e., 141 mha)
• 	Gross Irrigated Area = 121 mha (55% of Gross Cropped area i.e., 220 mha)
• 	Irrigation Intensity has increased to 155%.
Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY)
Objectives:
• 	Achieve 	convergence 	of 	investments 	in
irrigation at the field level
• 	Expand 	cultivable 	area 	under 	assured
irrigation
• 	Improve on-farm water use efficiency
• 	Enhance the adoption of micro-Irrigation
Components and implementing Ministries
• 	Per drop, More Crop: Ministry of Agriculture
• 	Har Khet ko Paani: Ministry of Jal Shakti
• 	Accelerated 	Irrigation 	Benefit 	Programme
(AIBP): Ministry of Jal Shakti
• 	Watershed 	Development 	Programme:
Ministry of Rural Development
Subsidy under the scheme
55 per cent financial assistance to small and
marginal 	farmers 	and 	45 	per 	cent 	to 	other
farmers for the installation of Drip and Sprinkler
systems

Practice MCQ No. 172
Consider the following statements related to Agricultural Credit:
1. 	The Agricultural Credit has steadily increased in the last decade.
2. 	The Share of long-term loans is higher than short term crop loans.
3. 	The Share of Institutional Credit is higher than Non-Institutional
Credit.
4. 	The Regional Rural Banks (RRBs) account for largest share of loans
to Agriculture.
Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only 	(b) 2 and 3 only (c) 1 and 3 only (d) 1, 3 and 4
Prelims Pointers on Agricultural Credit
• 	The Agricultural Credit has steadily increased in the last decade.
• 	Share of short-term loans is more than long term loans.
• 	Share of Institutional Credit is higher than Non-Institutional Credit.
• 	Scheduled Commercial Banks account for the largest share of agricultural loans
KISAN Credit Card
Objective: To meet the short term and long-term credit requirements of farmers
Eligibility: Cultivators + Tenant Farmers + Oral lessees + Sharecroppers + SHGs (Farmers involved in Fisheries and
Animal Husbandry are also eligible since 2018-19)
Modified Interest Subvention Scheme: (Development in Budget 2025)
Farmers received short-term loans of up to Rs.5 lakh (Earlier Rs 3 lakh) through KCC at a subsidized interest rate of
7%, with 1.5% interest subvention provided to eligible lending institutions. Additionally, farmers repaying loans
promptly are eligible for an incentive of up to 3% as Prompt Repayment Incentive (PRI) effectively reducing their
interest rate on KCC loans to 4%.
KISAN RIN Portal: Portal to enable farmers to avail subsidized credit under the Modified Intervention scheme

1. 	The KCC provide for only short-term crop loans to the farmers.
2. 	Farmers involved in fisheries and Animal husbandry are eligible to
receive benefits under KCC Scheme.
3. 	All the loans under the KCC enjoy the benefit of Interest rate

Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only (b) 2 only 	(c) 2 and 3 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 3
Agricultural Infrastructure Fund
Who will provide loans?: Commercial Banks, Cooperative Banks and NBFCs.
To 	whom 	loans 	will 	be 	provided?: 	Primary 	Agricultural 	Cooperative 	Societies, 	Farmers 	Producer
Organizations, Agriculture entrepreneurs, SHGs/JLGs, Start-ups, Govt. sponsored PPP Projects. APMCs are
also eligible since 2021.
For what purpose Loans will be provided?: Improve post-harvest Agri infrastructure such as Warehouses,
Cold chain, E-Commerce platforms, Collection Centres, Primary Processing Centres (PPCs) etc.
Are Secondary processing Units covered? The scheme is mainly limited to primary processing units.
However, certain selected Secondary processing activities like bread, Biscuits, Pasta etc. are also eligible.
Nature of Government’s support: Government’s support of up to Rs 2 lakh crores – March 2026 (Earlier Rs
1 lakh crores) for
• 	Interest Subvention: All loans up to a limit of Rs 2 crores will have interest subvention of 3% per
annum.
• 	Credit Guarantee for loans up to Rs 2 Crore.
Minimum and Maximum Limit on loans: No such limit. However, Benefits are restricted for loans of up to
Rs 2 crores only.
Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Agriculture and farmers’ welfare.
Nature of Scheme: Central Sector Scheme.
