2. 	The Share of long-term loans is higher than short term crop loans.
3. 	The Share of Institutional Credit is higher than Non-Institutional
Credit.
4. 	The Regional Rural Banks (RRBs) account for largest share of loans
to Agriculture.
Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only 	(b) 2 and 3 only (c) 1 and 3 only (d) 1, 3 and 4
Prelims Pointers on Agricultural Credit
• 	The Agricultural Credit has steadily increased in the last decade.
• 	Share of short-term loans is more than long term loans.
• 	Share of Institutional Credit is higher than Non-Institutional Credit.
• 	Scheduled Commercial Banks account for the largest share of agricultural loans
KISAN Credit Card
Objective: To meet the short term and long-term credit requirements of farmers
Eligibility: Cultivators + Tenant Farmers + Oral lessees + Sharecroppers + SHGs (Farmers involved in Fisheries and
Animal Husbandry are also eligible since 2018-19)
Modified Interest Subvention Scheme: (Development in Budget 2025)
Farmers received short-term loans of up to Rs.5 lakh (Earlier Rs 3 lakh) through KCC at a subsidized interest rate of
7%, with 1.5% interest subvention provided to eligible lending institutions. Additionally, farmers repaying loans
promptly are eligible for an incentive of up to 3% as Prompt Repayment Incentive (PRI) effectively reducing their
interest rate on KCC loans to 4%.
KISAN RIN Portal: Portal to enable farmers to avail subsidized credit under the Modified Intervention scheme
Practice MCQ No. 173
Consider the following statements related to Kisan Credit Card (KCC):
1. 	The KCC provide for only short-term crop loans to the farmers.
2. 	Farmers involved in fisheries and Animal husbandry are eligible to
receive benefits under KCC Scheme.
3. 	All the loans under the KCC enjoy the benefit of Interest rate
subvention.
Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only (b) 2 only 	(c) 2 and 3 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 3
Agricultural Infrastructure Fund
Who will provide loans?: Commercial Banks, Cooperative Banks and NBFCs.
To 	whom 	loans 	will 	be 	provided?: 	Primary 	Agricultural 	Cooperative 	Societies, 	Farmers 	Producer
Organizations, Agriculture entrepreneurs, SHGs/JLGs, Start-ups, Govt. sponsored PPP Projects. APMCs are
also eligible since 2021.
For what purpose Loans will be provided?: Improve post-harvest Agri infrastructure such as Warehouses,
Cold chain, E-Commerce platforms, Collection Centres, Primary Processing Centres (PPCs) etc.
Are Secondary processing Units covered? The scheme is mainly limited to primary processing units.
However, certain selected Secondary processing activities like bread, Biscuits, Pasta etc. are also eligible.
Nature of Government’s support: Government’s support of up to Rs 2 lakh crores – March 2026 (Earlier Rs
1 lakh crores) for
• 	Interest Subvention: All loans up to a limit of Rs 2 crores will have interest subvention of 3% per
annum.
• 	Credit Guarantee for loans up to Rs 2 Crore.
Minimum and Maximum Limit on loans: No such limit. However, Benefits are restricted for loans of up to
Rs 2 crores only.
Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Agriculture and farmers’ welfare.
Nature of Scheme: Central Sector Scheme.

2. 	Farmers involved in fisheries and Animal husbandry are eligible to
receive benefits under KCC Scheme.
3. 	All the loans under the KCC enjoy the benefit of Interest rate
subvention.
Which among the statements given above is/are correct?
(a) 1 and 2 only (b) 2 only 	(c) 2 and 3 only 	(d) 1, 2 and 3
Agricultural Infrastructure Fund
Who will provide loans?: Commercial Banks, Cooperative Banks and NBFCs.
To 	whom 	loans 	will 	be 	provided?: 	Primary 	Agricultural 	Cooperative 	Societies, 	Farmers 	Producer
Organizations, Agriculture entrepreneurs, SHGs/JLGs, Start-ups, Govt. sponsored PPP Projects. APMCs are
also eligible since 2021.
For what purpose Loans will be provided?: Improve post-harvest Agri infrastructure such as Warehouses,
Cold chain, E-Commerce platforms, Collection Centres, Primary Processing Centres (PPCs) etc.
Are Secondary processing Units covered? The scheme is mainly limited to primary processing units.
However, certain selected Secondary processing activities like bread, Biscuits, Pasta etc. are also eligible.
Nature of Government’s support: Government’s support of up to Rs 2 lakh crores – March 2026 (Earlier Rs
1 lakh crores) for
• 	Interest Subvention: All loans up to a limit of Rs 2 crores will have interest subvention of 3% per
annum.
• 	Credit Guarantee for loans up to Rs 2 Crore.
Minimum and Maximum Limit on loans: No such limit. However, Benefits are restricted for loans of up to
Rs 2 crores only.
Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Agriculture and farmers’ welfare.
Nature of Scheme: Central Sector Scheme.

Prelims Pointers on Agricultural Credit
• 	The Agricultural Credit has steadily increased in the last decade.
• 	Share of short-term loans is more than long term loans.
• 	Share of Institutional Credit is higher than Non-Institutional Credit.
• 	Scheduled Commercial Banks account for the largest share of agricultural loans
KISAN Credit Card
Objective: To meet the short term and long-term credit requirements of farmers
Eligibility: Cultivators + Tenant Farmers + Oral lessees + Sharecroppers + SHGs (Farmers involved in Fisheries and
Animal Husbandry are also eligible since 2018-19)
Modified Interest Subvention Scheme: (Development in Budget 2025)
Farmers received short-term loans of up to Rs.5 lakh (Earlier Rs 3 lakh) through KCC at a subsidized interest rate of
7%, with 1.5% interest subvention provided to eligible lending institutions. Additionally, farmers repaying loans
promptly are eligible for an incentive of up to 3% as Prompt Repayment Incentive (PRI) effectively reducing their
interest rate on KCC loans to 4%.
KISAN RIN Portal: Portal to enable farmers to avail subsidized credit under the Modified Intervention scheme

context:
By: Basava Uppin
Sl. No 	Sustainable Agricultural
Practice/Systems
Prelims Pointers
8. 	Contour Farming and Terrace
Farming
Definition: In Contour Farming, crops are planted along the natural contours of gently sloping land. Terrace farming involves the
creation of stepped levels or terraces on steep slopes, creating flat areas for cultivation.
Suitability: Contour farming is suitable for gently sloping land while Terrace farming is suitable for steep slopes.
Method: Both Contour farming and Terrace Farming prevent soil erosion and surface run-off. Contour farming does this by planting
crops along the contour lines of slope or perpendicular to slope while Terrace Farming does this by creating step-like terraces.
9. 	Vertical Farming (Prelims 2020) 	Vertical farming is the practice of growing crops or vegetables in vertically stacked layers in a controlled environment. Components:
Hydroponics: Growing plants in nutrient solutions that are free of soil leading to higher yields.
Aeroponics: In aeroponics, there is no growing medium. Mist or nutrient solutions are sprayed on roots and plants instead of water.
Requires very less space, very less water and no soil.
Advantages: Higher Yields + Less space + Less water + Crops can be cultivated year around + Promote agriculture in Urban areas.
10. 	Soil Solarization 	Practice used to manage weeds, pests and insects in soil. The soil surface is covered with plastic, which allows sunlight to pass
through and heat up the soil. As the temperature of soil increases, it can kill the pests and insects in the soil.
12. 	Saguna Rice technique 	Unique new method of cultivation of rice and related rotation crops without ploughing, puddling and transplanting (rice) on
permanent raised beds. Features:
1. 	No-Till Farming: Soil is not ploughed or disturbed before planting. This helps to maintain soil structure, reduce erosion,
and improve soil health.
2. 	Permanent Raised beds: Rice is cultivated on permanent raised beds with furrows in between to facilitate irrigation and
drainage.
3. 	No Transplantation: Seeds are directly sown into the soil, eliminating the need for transplanting seedlings. This reduces
labor and water requirements.
4. 	Reduced water consumption as compared to Traditional flood irrigation method. The furrows between the beds help to
manage water efficiently.
13. 	Floating Farming 	Method of producing food using land that has been submerged for a long period. It is mainly aimed at adapting cultivation to
increased or prolonged flooding. The approach employs beds of rotting vegetation, which act as compost for crop growth. These
beds are able to float on the surface of the water, thus creating areas of land suitable for agriculture within waterlogged regions.
14. 	Tensiometer (Prelims 2020) 	A tensiometer is a tool for monitoring soil moisture conditions in agriculture. This measurement helps farmers make informed
decisions about irrigation scheduling, ensuring that crops receive the right amount of water at the right time.

Method: Both Contour farming and Terrace Farming prevent soil erosion and surface run-off. Contour farming does this by planting
crops along the contour lines of slope or perpendicular to slope while Terrace Farming does this by creating step-like terraces.
9. 	Vertical Farming (Prelims 2020) 	Vertical farming is the practice of growing crops or vegetables in vertically stacked layers in a controlled environment. Components:
Hydroponics: Growing plants in nutrient solutions that are free of soil leading to higher yields.
Aeroponics: In aeroponics, there is no growing medium. Mist or nutrient solutions are sprayed on roots and plants instead of water.
Requires very less space, very less water and no soil.
Advantages: Higher Yields + Less space + Less water + Crops can be cultivated year around + Promote agriculture in Urban areas.
10. 	Soil Solarization 	Practice used to manage weeds, pests and insects in soil. The soil surface is covered with plastic, which allows sunlight to pass
through and heat up the soil. As the temperature of soil increases, it can kill the pests and insects in the soil.
12. 	Saguna Rice technique 	Unique new method of cultivation of rice and related rotation crops without ploughing, puddling and transplanting (rice) on
permanent raised beds. Features:
1. 	No-Till Farming: Soil is not ploughed or disturbed before planting. This helps to maintain soil structure, reduce erosion,
and improve soil health.
2. 	Permanent Raised beds: Rice is cultivated on permanent raised beds with furrows in between to facilitate irrigation and
drainage.
3. 	No Transplantation: Seeds are directly sown into the soil, eliminating the need for transplanting seedlings. This reduces
labor and water requirements.
4. 	Reduced water consumption as compared to Traditional flood irrigation method. The furrows between the beds help to
manage water efficiently.
13. 	Floating Farming 	Method of producing food using land that has been submerged for a long period. It is mainly aimed at adapting cultivation to
increased or prolonged flooding. The approach employs beds of rotting vegetation, which act as compost for crop growth. These
beds are able to float on the surface of the water, thus creating areas of land suitable for agriculture within waterlogged regions.
14. 	Tensiometer (Prelims 2020) 	A tensiometer is a tool for monitoring soil moisture conditions in agriculture. This measurement helps farmers make informed
decisions about irrigation scheduling, ensuring that crops receive the right amount of water at the right time.

By: Basava Uppin	Ports, Shipping and Waterways
Present Status: India has 13 Major ports and about 200 non-major
ports. Approximately 95% of the country’s trade by volume and 68%
by value is moved through Maritime Transport.
Definition of Major Ports:
• 	Major Ports are defined in Section 3(8) of the Indian Ports act,
1908 to mean any port which the Central Government may by
notification in the Official Gazette declare.
• 	Major ports come under the control of Central Government while
the Minor ports come under control of respective states.
Some important Prelims pointers about Ports:
Galathea Bay in Great Nicobar: Declared as India’s 13th Major Port.
Vizhinjam Port: India’s first deep water Container Transhipment Port
built by Kerala Govt in PPP mode with Adani Group.
Vadhvan Port in Palghar District of Maharashtra: To be built by JNPT
and Maharashtra Maritime Board (MMB) as a Landlord Port.
Kamarajar Port: India’s only Major corporate Port as it is registered as
companies under Companies Act while other Ports are registered as
Trusts.
Deendayal Port: Overtaken Paradip port to become India’s largest
cargo handling port in 2025-26.
New Mangalore Port: 100% Solar Powered Port.
Tuticorin Port:
• 	Transhipment Hub on the East Coast
• 	India’s first Hydrogen Hub Port.
Shyamaprasad Mookerjee Port: Only Riverine Major Port.
Jawaharlal Nehru Port: India’s first 100% landlord port

By: Basava Uppin	Shipping Building Industry
Ship Building Industry
Shipbuilding Financial Assistance Policy (SBFAP):
• 	Financial Assistance in the range of 20% to 11% of the value of ships to shipyards for building
shipping fleet in India.
• 	The scheme was supposed to end on March 2026. However, the scheme has been extended
until March 31, 2036.
• 	It also includes the Ship breaking Credit note which provides for credit of 40% of the ship's
scrap value. National Ship building mission will be established to oversee the implementation.
Large ships above a specified size will be included in the infrastructure harmonized master list
(HML)
Maritime development Fund: Corpus of Rs 25,000 crores. 49 per cent contribution by the
Government, and the balance will be mobilized from ports and private sector. Provide financing
for ship acquisition in the form of equity or debt.
Shipbuilding Development Scheme (SbDS): Develop 8 Mega ship building clusters such as
Dugarajapatnam (AP).
Right of First Refusal Policy: Under this regime, Indian shipowners are given priority over foreign
competitors when it comes to chartering vessels for cargo transport within Indian waters. If a
foreign shipowner submits a bid for a contract, Indian shipowners are given the opportunity to
match the offer and secure the contract
Coastal Shipping Act, 2025
• 	No need to get license for Indian vessels for coastal shipping
• 	Allows Riverine vessels to operate in Coastal Waters and vice-versa and thus reduces the costly
and time-consuming transhipment
Merchant Shipping Act, 2025
Change in Definition of Indian Shipping Vessel: Earlier, Indian Shipping Vessel meant vessels
wholly owned by Indians. Now, Indian Shipping Vessels would mean vessels "partly" owned by
Indians. The percentage of ownership to be notified by the Government. Thus it enables the NRIs,
OCIs, Foreign entities to have ownership in the shipping vessels registered in India.
